From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

East Asian ethnic group

"Han people" redirects here. For citizens and residents of [China](/wiki/China "China"), see [Chinese people](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people"). For the homophonic Han people of Korea, see [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans"). For the Alaskan people, see [Hän](/wiki/H%C3%A4n "Hän").

For the language of Han Chinese, see [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"). For the Chinese imperial dynasty, see [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty").

The **Han Chinese**, alternatively **Han people**[[a]](#cite_note-Han_people-18) or **Chinese people**,[[18]](#cite_note-19) are an [East Asian ethnic group](/wiki/East_Asian_people "East Asian people") native to [Greater China](/wiki/Greater_China "Greater China").[[19]](#cite_note-20) With a global population of over 1.4 billion, the Han Chinese are the [world's largest ethnic group](/wiki/List_of_contemporary_ethnic_groups "List of contemporary ethnic groups"), making up about 17% of the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population"). The Han Chinese represent 91.11% of the population in [China](/wiki/China "China") and 97% of the population in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").[[20]](#cite_note-中華民國國情簡介2016-21)[[21]](#cite_note-Yearbook2014-22) They are a significant [diasporic group](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") in Southeast Asian countries such as [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"),[[22]](#cite_note-23) [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").

The Han Chinese have exerted a primary formative influence in [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture") and [history](/wiki/History_of_China "History of China").[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-hack-25)[[25]](#cite_note-MA-26) Originating from [Zhongyuan](/wiki/Zhongyuan "Zhongyuan"), the Han Chinese trace their ancestry and culture to the [Huaxia](/wiki/Huaxia "Huaxia") people, a confederation of agricultural tribes that lived along the middle and lower reaches of the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River")[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-CH-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) in the north central plains of China.[[30]](#cite_note-CA-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

Han Chinese people and culture later spread southwards in the Chinese mainland, driven by large and sustained waves of migration during successive periods of Chinese history, for example the [Qin](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC) and [Han](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (202 BC – 220 AD) dynasties, leading to a demographic and economic tilt towards the south, and the absorption of various [non-Han ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Chinese_history#List_of_ethnic_groups "Ethnic groups in Chinese history") over the centuries at various points in Chinese history.[[28]](#cite_note-CH-29)[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The Han Chinese became the main inhabitants of the fertile lowland areas and cities of Southern China by the time of the Tang and Song dynasties,[[34]](#cite_note-PK-35) with minority tribes occupying the highlands.

## Identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Identity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Aoqun_1.jpg/250px-Aoqun_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aoqun_1.jpg)

A girl wearing *[ruqun](/wiki/Ruqun "Ruqun")*, one of the most distinctive set of attire of female [hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Daopao4.jpg/250px-Daopao4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daopao4.jpg)

A man wearing *[daopao](/wiki/Daopao "Daopao")*, one of the most distinctive set of attire of male [hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")

The term "Han" not only refers to a specific ethnic collective, but also points to a shared ancestry, history, and cultural [identity](/wiki/Group_identity "Group identity"). The term "Huaxia" was used by the ancient Chinese philosopher [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius")'s contemporaries during the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") to elucidate the shared ethnicity of all Chinese;[[35]](#cite_note-Brindley2015-36) Chinese people called themselves "Hua people" (华人; 華人; *Huárén*).[[36]](#cite_note-PandeyGeschiere2003-37)

The Warring States period led to the emergence of the Zhou-era Chinese referring to themselves as being Huaxia (literally 'the beautiful grandeur'): under the [Hua–Yi distinction](/wiki/Hua%E2%80%93Yi_distinction "Hua–Yi distinction"), a "Hua" culture (often translated as 'civilized') was contrasted to that of peoples perceived as "Yi" (often translated as '[barbarian](/wiki/Barbarian#China "Barbarian")') living on the peripheries of the Zhou kingdoms.[[37]](#cite_note-EC-38)[[28]](#cite_note-CH-29)[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)

[Overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") who possess non-Chinese citizenship are commonly referred as *Huaren* or *Huazu* (华族; 華族; *Huázú*). The two respective aforementioned terms are applied solely to those with a Han background that is semantically distinct from *Zhongguo ren* (中国人; 中國人) which has connotations and implications limited to being [citizens](/wiki/Chinese_passport "Chinese passport") and [nationals](/wiki/Chinese_nationality_law "Chinese nationality law") of China, especially with regard to [ethnic minorities in China](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities_in_China "Ethnic minorities in China").[[40]](#cite_note-chang-41)[[41]](#cite_note-sheng-42)[[24]](#cite_note-hack-25)

## Designation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Designation")]

### "Han people"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: \"Han people\"")]

The name "Han people" (漢人; 汉人; *Hànrén*) first appeared during the [Northern and Southern period](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_period "Northern and Southern period") and was inspired by the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), which is considered to be one of the first [golden ages in Chinese history](/wiki/Golden_ages_of_China "Golden ages of China"). As a unified and cohesive empire that succeeded the short-lived Qin dynasty, Han China established itself as the center of the East Asian geopolitical order at the time, projecting its power and influence unto Asian neighbors. It was comparable with the contemporary [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") in population size, geographical extent, and cultural reach.[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-EG-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45) The Han dynasty's prestige and prominence led many of the ancient Huaxia to identify themselves as 'Han people'.[[37]](#cite_note-EC-38)[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-HD-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) Similarly, the [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") also came to be named and alluded to as the "Han language" (漢語; 汉语; *Hànyǔ*) ever since and the Chinese script is referred to as "[Han characters](/wiki/Han_characters "Han characters")".[[43]](#cite_note-EG-44)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[47]](#cite_note-HD-48)

The word "Han" (漢/汉) in its original poetic meaning found in ancient Chinese works such as the *[Classic of Poetry](/wiki/Classic_of_Poetry "Classic of Poetry")*, refers to the "Milky Way."[[b]](#cite_note-53)

### *Huaren* and *Huayi*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Huaren and Huayi")]

Prior to the Han dynasty, Chinese scholars used the term *[Huaxia](/wiki/Huaxia "Huaxia")* (華夏; 华夏) in texts to describe [China proper](/wiki/China_proper "China proper"), while the Chinese populace were referred to as either the 'various Hua' (諸華; 诸华; *Zhūhuá*) or 'various Xia' (诸夏; 諸夏; *Zhūxià*). This gave rise to two term commonly used nowadays by [Overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") as an ethnic identity for the Chinese diaspora – *Huaren* (華人; 华人; *Huárén*; 'ethnic Chinese people') and *Huaqiao* (华侨; 華僑; *Huáqiáo*; 'the Chinese immigrant'), meaning [Overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese").[[24]](#cite_note-hack-25) It has also given rise to the literary [name for China](/wiki/Name_for_China "Name for China") – *Zhonghua* (中華; 中华; *Zhōnghuá*).[[25]](#cite_note-MA-26) While the general term *Zhongguo ren* (中國人; 中国人) refers to any [Chinese citizen](/wiki/Chinese_citizen "Chinese citizen") or [Chinese national](/wiki/Chinese_national "Chinese national") regardless of their ethnic origins and does not necessary imply Han ancestry, the term *huaren* in its narrow, classical usages implies Central Plains or Han ancestry.[[41]](#cite_note-sheng-42)

### *Tangren*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tangren")]

Among some Southern Han Chinese varieties such as [Cantonese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese"), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") and [Minnan](/wiki/Southern_Min "Southern Min"), the term *Tangren* (唐人; *Tángrén*; 'people of Tang'), derived from the name of the later [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (618–907) that oversaw what is regarded as another [golden age of China](/wiki/Golden_age_of_China "Golden age of China"). The self-identification as *Tangren* is popular in south China, because it was at this time that massive waves of migration and settlement led to a shift in the center of gravity of the Chinese nation, away from the tumult of the Central Plains to the peaceful lands south of the Yangtze and on the southeastern coast.[[52]](#cite_note-Linguistic_landscape_and_heritage_l-54)

This led to the earnest settlement by Chinese of lands previously regarded as part of the empire's sparsely populated frontier or periphery. Guangdong and Fujian, hitherto regarded as backwater regions, were populated by the descendants of garrison soldiers, exiles and refugees, became new centers and representatives of Han Chinese culture under the influence of the new Han migrants. The term is used in everyday colloquial discourse and is also an element in one of the words for [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown "Chinatown"): 'streets of Tang people' (唐人街; *Tángrénjiē*; *Tong4 jan4 gaai1*).[[52]](#cite_note-Linguistic_landscape_and_heritage_l-54) The phrase *Huábù* (華埠; 华埠) is also used to refer to Chinatowns.

### *Zhonghua minzu*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Zhonghua minzu")]

The term *[Zhonghua minzu](/wiki/Zhonghua_minzu "Zhonghua minzu")*, literally the 'Chinese nation', currently used as a supra-ethnic concept publicised first by the Republic of China, then by the People's Republic of China, was historically used specifically to refer to the Han Chinese. In his article "Observations on the Chinese ethnic groups in History", [Liang Qichao](/wiki/Liang_Qichao "Liang Qichao"), who coined the term *Zhonghua minzu*, wrote "the present-day *Zhonghua minzu* refers to what is commonly known as the Han Chinese".[[c]](#cite_note-55)[[53]](#cite_note-56) It was only after the founding of the Society for the National Great Unity of the Republic of China[[d]](#cite_note-57) in 1912 that the term began to officially include ethnic minorities from all regions in China.[[54]](#cite_note-58)[[55]](#cite_note-59)

## Han Chinese subgroups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Han Chinese subgroups")]

Han Chinese can be divided into subgroups, based on the variety of Chinese that they speak.[[56]](#cite_note-Genetic_Studies_of_Human_Diversity-60)[[57]](#cite_note-Ancient_DNA_Reveals_That_the_Geneti-61) Waves of migration have occurred throughout China's long history and vast geographical expanse, engendering the emergence of Han Chinese subgroups found throughout the regions of modern China today, with distinct regional features.[[58]](#cite_note-Gernet1996-62)[[59]](#cite_note-gene-63)[[60]](#cite_note-Vickers2013-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82_%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B9%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%B2_%D0%B2_%D0%9A%D0%9D%D0%A0.png/250px-%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82_%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B9%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%B2_%D0%B2_%D0%9A%D0%9D%D0%A0.png)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D1%80%D0%BE%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82_%D0%BA%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B9%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%B2_%D0%B2_%D0%9A%D0%9D%D0%A0.png)

The percentage of Han Chinese by county level, including [Mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau")

The expansion of the Han people outside their linguistic homeland in the Yellow River is an important part of their historical consciousness and ethnogenesis, and accounts for their present-day diversity.

There were several periods of mass migration of Han people to Southeastern and Southern China throughout history.[[58]](#cite_note-Gernet1996-62) Initially, the sparsely populated regions of south China were inhabited by tribes known only as the Bai Yue or Hundred Yue. Many of these tribes developed into kingdoms under rulers and nobility of Han Chinese ethnicity, but retained a Bai Yue majority for several centuries.[[61]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-65)[[62]](#cite_note-scribd.com-66)

Others were forcibly brought into the Sinosphere by the imperial ambitions of emperors such as Qin Shi Huangdi and Han Wu Di, both of whom settled hundreds of thousands of Chinese in these lands to form agricultural colonies and military garrisons. Even then, control over these lands was tenuous, and Bai Yue cultural identity remained strong until sustained waves of Han Chinese emigration in the Jin, Tang and Song dynasties altered the demographic balance completely.[[61]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-65)[[62]](#cite_note-scribd.com-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Map_of_sinitic_languages_full-en.svg/250px-Map_of_sinitic_languages_full-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_sinitic_languages_full-en.svg)

The distribution of Chinese languages in China

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Mandarin_subgroups_and_Jin_group.png/250px-Mandarin_subgroups_and_Jin_group.png)](/wiki/File:Mandarin_subgroups_and_Jin_group.png)

The distribution of Mandarin dialects in China

Chinese language (or Chinese languages) can be divided to 10 primary dialects (or languages).[[63]](#cite_note-67)

Each [Han Chinese subgroup](/wiki/Han_Chinese_subgroups "Han Chinese subgroups") (民系) can be identified through their dialects:[[56]](#cite_note-Genetic_Studies_of_Human_Diversity-60)[[57]](#cite_note-Ancient_DNA_Reveals_That_the_Geneti-61)

* [Wu](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") (吴语): [Jiangzhe people](/wiki/Wu_Chinese-speaking_people "Wu Chinese-speaking people") (江浙民系)
* [Hui](/wiki/Huizhou_Chinese "Huizhou Chinese") (徽语): [Wannan people](/wiki/Huizhou,_Anhui#Culture "Huizhou, Anhui") (皖南民系)
* [Gan](/wiki/Gan_Chinese "Gan Chinese") (赣语): [Jiangxi people](/wiki/Gan_Chinese-speaking_people "Gan Chinese-speaking people") (江西民系)
* [Xiang](/wiki/Xiang_Chinese "Xiang Chinese") (湘语): [Hunan people](/wiki/Hunanese_people "Hunanese people") (湖南民系)
* [Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese "Min Chinese") (闽语): [Minhai people](/wiki/Min_Chinese_speakers "Min Chinese speakers") (闽海民系)
* [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") (客语): [Hakka people](/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people") (客家民系)
* [Yue](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") (粤语): [Cantonese people](/wiki/Cantonese_people "Cantonese people") (岭南民系)
* [Pinghua](/wiki/Pinghua "Pinghua") (平话) and [Tuhua](/wiki/Tuhua_(Chinese_dialect) "Tuhua (Chinese dialect)") (土话): Pingnan people (平南民系)[[64]](#cite_note-68)[[65]](#cite_note-69)[[66]](#cite_note-70)[[67]](#cite_note-71)[[68]](#cite_note-72)
* [Jin](/wiki/Jin_Chinese "Jin Chinese") (晋语): [Jinsui people](/wiki/Shanxi#Culture "Shanxi") (晋绥民系)
* [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (官话): Northern people (北方民系)[[69]](#cite_note-73)
  + [Northeastern](/wiki/Northeastern_Mandarin "Northeastern Mandarin") (东北): [Northeastern people](/wiki/Northeast_China#Culture "Northeast China") (东北民系)
  + [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing_Mandarin_(division_of_Mandarin) "Beijing Mandarin (division of Mandarin)") (北平): [Youyan people](/wiki/Beijing#Culture "Beijing") (幽燕民系)
  + [Jilu](/wiki/Jilu_Mandarin "Jilu Mandarin") (冀鲁): [Jilu people](/wiki/Qilu_culture#Language "Qilu culture") (冀鲁民系)
  + [Jiaoliao](/wiki/Jiaoliao_Mandarin "Jiaoliao Mandarin") (胶辽): [Jiaoliao people](/wiki/Qilu_culture#Language "Qilu culture") (胶辽民系)
  + [Central Plains](/wiki/Central_Plains_Mandarin "Central Plains Mandarin") (中原): [Central Plains people](/wiki/Zhongyuan_culture "Zhongyuan culture") (中原民系)
  + [Lanyin](/wiki/Lanyin_Mandarin "Lanyin Mandarin") (兰银): [Longyou people](/wiki/Gansu#Culture "Gansu") (陇右民系)
  + [Southwestern](/wiki/Southwestern_Mandarin "Southwestern Mandarin") (西南): [Southwestern people](/wiki/Sichuanese_people "Sichuanese people") (西南民系)
  + [Jianghuai](/wiki/Lower_Yangtze_Mandarin "Lower Yangtze Mandarin") (江淮): [Jianghuai people](/wiki/Subei_people "Subei people") (江淮民系)

### Military garrisons and agricultural colonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Military garrisons and agricultural colonies")]

The first emperor [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") is said to have sent several hundred thousand men and fifteen thousand women to form agricultural and military settlements in [Lingnan](/wiki/Lingnan "Lingnan") (present day [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") and Guangdong), under the leadership of a general named Zhao Tuo. The famous Han emperor, Han Wu Di, ordered another two hundred thousand men to build ships to attack and colonialize the Lingnan region, adding to the population in Guangdong and Guangxi.[[58]](#cite_note-Gernet1996-62)

The first urban conurbations in the region, for example, Panyu, were created by Han settlers rather than the Bai Yue, who preferred to maintain small settlements subsisting on swidden agriculture and rice farming. Later on, Guangdong, northern Vietnam, and Yunnan all experienced a surge in Han Chinese migrants during [Wang Mang](/wiki/Wang_Mang "Wang Mang")'s reign.[[58]](#cite_note-Gernet1996-62) The demographic composition and culture of these regions during this period, could however scarcely be said to have been Sinitic outside the confines of these agricultural settlements and military outposts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Historical southward migrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Historical southward migrations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Han_Expansion.png/250px-Han_Expansion.png)](/wiki/File:Han_Expansion.png)

A map showing the [southward expansion of the Han dynasty](/wiki/Southward_expansion_of_the_Han_dynasty "Southward expansion of the Han dynasty") during the 2nd century BC

The genesis of the modern Han people and their subgroups cannot be understood apart from their historical migrations to the south, resulting in a depopulation of the Central Plains, a fission between those that remained and those that headed south, and their subsequent fusion with aboriginal tribes south of the Yangtze, even as the centres of Han Chinese culture and wealth moved from the Yellow River Basin to Jiangnan, and to a lesser extent also, to Fujian and Guangdong.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At various points in Chinese history, collapses of central authority in the face of barbarian uprisings or invasions and the loss of control of the Chinese heartland triggered mass migratory waves which transformed the demographic composition and cultural identity of the south. This process of sustained mass migration has been known as "garments and headdresses moving south" 衣冠南渡 (yì guān nán dù), on account of it first being led by the aristocratic classes.[[70]](#cite_note-74)[[34]](#cite_note-PK-35)[[71]](#cite_note-SK-75)

Such migratory waves were numerous and triggered by such events such as the [Uprising of the Five Barbarians](/wiki/Uprising_of_the_Five_Barbarians "Uprising of the Five Barbarians") during the Jin dynasty (304–316 AD) in which China was completely overrun by minority groups previously serving as vassals and servants to Sima (the royal house of Jin), the [An Lu Shan rebellion](/wiki/An_Lu_Shan_rebellion "An Lu Shan rebellion") during the Tang dynasty (755–763 AD), and the [Jingkang incident](/wiki/Jingkang_incident "Jingkang incident") (1127 AD) and [Jin-Song wars](/wiki/Jin%E2%80%93Song_wars "Jin–Song wars"). These events caused widespread devastation, and even depopulated the north, resulting in the complete social and political breakdown and collapse of central authority in the Central Plains, triggering massive, sustained waves of Han Chinese migration into South China,[[72]](#cite_note-HT-76)[[73]](#cite_note-77) leading to the formation of distinct Han lineages,[[74]](#cite_note-Clark2007-78) who also likely assimilated the by-now partially sinicized Bai Yue in their midst.

Modern Han Chinese subgroups, such as the [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese_people "Cantonese people"), the [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people"), the [Henghua](/wiki/Putian_people "Putian people"), the [Hainanese](/wiki/Hainanese "Hainanese"), the [Hoklo](/wiki/Hoklo_people "Hoklo people") peoples, the [Gan](/wiki/Gan_Chinese "Gan Chinese"), the [Xiang](/wiki/Xiang_Chinese "Xiang Chinese"), the [Wu-speaking](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") peoples, all claim Han Chinese ancestry pointing to official histories and their own genealogical records to support such claims.[[34]](#cite_note-PK-35)[[71]](#cite_note-SK-75)[[75]](#cite_note-EisenstadtSchluchter-79)[[76]](#cite_note-LeongWright1997-80) Linguists hypothesize that proto- Wu and Min varieties of Chinese may have originated from the time of [Jin](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)"),[[77]](#cite_note-81) while the proto- Yue and Hakka varieties perhaps from the [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") and [Song](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), about half-a-millennium later.[[78]](#cite_note-82) The presence of [Tai-Kradai](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages "Kra–Dai languages") substrates in these dialects may have been due to the assimilation of the remaining groups of Bai Yue, integrating these lands into the Sinosphere proper.

#### First wave: Jin dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: First wave: Jin dynasty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Wu_Hu_Uprising.png/250px-Wu_Hu_Uprising.png)](/wiki/File:Wu_Hu_Uprising.png)

The major nomadic groups involved in the [Uprising of the Five Barbarians](/wiki/Uprising_of_the_Five_Barbarians "Uprising of the Five Barbarians")

The chaos of the [Uprising of the Five Barbarians](/wiki/Upheaval_of_the_Five_Barbarians "Upheaval of the Five Barbarians") triggered the first massive movement of Han Chinese dominated by civilians rather than soldiers to the south, being led principally by the aristocracy and the Jin elite. Thus, Jiangnan, comprising Hangzhou's coastal regions and the Yangtze valley were settled in the 4th century AD by families descended from Chinese nobility.[[58]](#cite_note-Gernet1996-62)[[79]](#cite_note-LagerweyLü2009_34-83)

Special "[commanderies](/wiki/Commandery_(China) "Commandery (China)") of immigrants" and "white registers" were created for the massive number of Han Chinese immigrating during this period [[58]](#cite_note-Gernet1996-62) which included notable families such as the Wang and the Xie.[[80]](#cite_note-84) A religious group known as the [Celestial Masters](/wiki/Celestial_Masters "Celestial Masters") contributed to the movement. Jiangnan became the most populous and prosperous region of China.[[81]](#cite_note-85)[[82]](#cite_note-86)

The [Uprising of the Five Barbarians](/wiki/Upheaval_of_the_Five_Barbarians "Upheaval of the Five Barbarians"), also led to the resettlement of Fujian. The province of Fujian - whose aboriginal inhabitants had been deported to the Central Plains by Han Wu Di, was now repopulated by Han Chinese settlers and colonists from the Chinese heartland. The "Eight Great Surnames" were eight noble families who migrated from the Central Plains to Fujian - these were the Hu, He, Qiu, Dan, Zheng, Huang, Chen and Lin clans, who remain there until this very day.[[83]](#cite_note-87)[[84]](#cite_note-88)[[85]](#cite_note-89)[[86]](#cite_note-90)

#### Tang dynasty and An Lushan rebellion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tang dynasty and An Lushan rebellion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/%E5%94%90%E5%B0%9A%E7%88%B6%E6%B1%BE%E9%98%B3%E5%BF%A0%E6%AD%A6%E7%8E%8B%E5%83%8F.jpg/250px-%E5%94%90%E5%B0%9A%E7%88%B6%E6%B1%BE%E9%98%B3%E5%BF%A0%E6%AD%A6%E7%8E%8B%E5%83%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%94%90%E5%B0%9A%E7%88%B6%E6%B1%BE%E9%98%B3%E5%BF%A0%E6%AD%A6%E7%8E%8B%E5%83%8F.jpg)

[Guo Ziyi](/wiki/Guo_Ziyi "Guo Ziyi"), who quelled the An Lushan rebellion, which caused a fall in recorded numbers of Tang inhabitants from 53 million to 17 million people

In the wake of the [An Lushan rebellion](/wiki/An_Lushan_rebellion "An Lushan rebellion"), a further wave of Han migrants from northern China headed the south.[[60]](#cite_note-Vickers2013-64)[[72]](#cite_note-HT-76)[[34]](#cite_note-PK-35)[[71]](#cite_note-SK-75)[[87]](#cite_note-Clark2007_2-91) At the start of the rebellion in 755 there were 52.9 million registered inhabitants of the Tang Empire, and after its end in 764, only 16.9 million were recorded.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is likely that the difference in census figures was due to the complete breakdown in administrative capabilities, as well as the widespread escape from the north by the Han Chinese and their mass migration to the south.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By now, the Han Chinese population in the south far outstripped that of the Bai Yue. Guangdong and Fujian both experienced a significant influx of Northern Han Chinese settlers, leading many Cantonese, Hokkien and Teochew individuals to identify themselves as *Tangren*, which has served as a means to assert and acknowledge their ethnic and cultural origin and identity.[[88]](#cite_note-Wilkinson2000-92)

#### Jin–Song wars and Mongol invasion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Jin–Song wars and Mongol invasion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/%E5%B2%B3%E9%A3%9E%E5%83%8F.jpg/250px-%E5%B2%B3%E9%A3%9E%E5%83%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%B2%B3%E9%A3%9E%E5%83%8F.jpg)

[Yue Fei](/wiki/Yue_Fei "Yue Fei"), who was a general of the Song dynasty against the Jurchen (Jin) invasion, is considered a Chinese national hero and is often worshipped alongside [Guan Yu](/wiki/Guan_Yu "Guan Yu") in [martial temples](/wiki/Martial_temple "Martial temple").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Song_Jin_Wars.jpg/250px-Song_Jin_Wars.jpg)](/wiki/File:Song_Jin_Wars.jpg)

The Jin–Song wars resulted in the devastation of Northern China and the displacement of more Han Chinese to Southern China

The [Jin–Song Wars](/wiki/Jin%E2%80%93Song_Wars "Jin–Song Wars") caused yet another wave of mass migration of the Han Chinese from Northern China to Southern China,[[34]](#cite_note-PK-35)[[71]](#cite_note-SK-75) leading to a further increase in the Han Chinese population across southern Chinese provinces. The formation of the Hainanese and Hakka people can be attributed to the chaos of this period.

The [Mongol conquest of China](/wiki/Mongol_conquest_of_China "Mongol conquest of China") during the thirteenth century once again caused a surging influx of Northern Han Chinese refugees to move south to settle and develop the [Pearl River Delta](/wiki/Pearl_River_Delta "Pearl River Delta").[[89]](#cite_note-China:_An_Environmental_History-93)[[90]](#cite_note-Lexington_Books-94)[[91]](#cite_note-Cambridge_University_Press-95)[[92]](#cite_note-Rowman_Altamira-96) These mass migrations over the centuries inevitably led to the demographic expansion, economic prosperity, agricultural advancements, and cultural flourishing of Southern China, which remained relatively peaceful unlike its northern counterpart.[[93]](#cite_note-Springer-97)[[94]](#cite_note-Six_Dynasties-98)[[95]](#cite_note-MS-99)[[96]](#cite_note-QC-100)[[97]](#cite_note-China:_A_New_Cultural_History-101)[[98]](#cite_note-The_History_of_China-102)[[99]](#cite_note-books.google.com-103)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Distribution")]

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Hanfu_yueyao.jpg/250px-Hanfu_yueyao.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hanfu_yueyao.jpg)

Modern Han Chinese men wearing *[hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")*.

Main articles: [Chinese people](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people"), [Demographics of China](/wiki/Demographics_of_China "Demographics of China"), and [Han Chinese subgroups](/wiki/Han_Chinese_subgroups "Han Chinese subgroups")

The vast majority of Han Chinese – over 1.2 billion – live in the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC), where they constitute about 90% of its overall population.[[100]](#cite_note-ciastat2018-104) Han Chinese in China have been a culturally, economically and politically dominant majority vis-à-vis the non-Han minorities throughout most of China's recorded history.[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-Chua_2000_328-106) Han Chinese are almost the majority in every [Chinese province, municipality and autonomous region](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_China "Administrative divisions of China") except for the autonomous regions of [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang") (38% or 40% in 2010) and [Tibet Autonomous Region](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region") (8% in 2014), where [Uighurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") and [Tibetans](/wiki/Tibetan_people "Tibetan people") are the majority, respectively.

#### Hong Kong and Macau

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Hong Kong and Macau")]

Main articles: [Hongkongers](/wiki/Hongkongers "Hongkongers"), [Macau people](/wiki/Macau_people "Macau people"), [Demographics of Hong Kong](/wiki/Demographics_of_Hong_Kong "Demographics of Hong Kong"), and [Demographics of Macau](/wiki/Demographics_of_Macau "Demographics of Macau")

Han Chinese also constitute the majority in both of the [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") of the PRC.[[103]](#cite_note-107)[[104]](#cite_note-108)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The Han Chinese in Hong Kong and Macau have been culturally, economically and politically dominant majority vis-à-vis the non-Han minorities.[[102]](#cite_note-Chua_2000_328-106)[[105]](#cite_note-Chua_2003_https://archive.org/details/worldonfirehowex00chua_0/page/178_178-109)

### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

Main articles: [Han Taiwanese](/wiki/Han_Taiwanese "Han Taiwanese"), [Taiwanese people](/wiki/Taiwanese_people "Taiwanese people"), and [Demographics of Taiwan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Taiwan "Demographics of Taiwan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/%E8%89%8B%E8%88%BA%E9%BE%8D%E5%B1%B1%E5%AF%BA_%E8%87%BA%E5%8C%97%E5%B8%82_%E7%9B%B4%E8%BD%84%E5%B8%82%E5%AE%9A%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F%E5%AF%BA%E5%BB%9F_Venation_2.JPG/250px-%E8%89%8B%E8%88%BA%E9%BE%8D%E5%B1%B1%E5%AF%BA_%E8%87%BA%E5%8C%97%E5%B8%82_%E7%9B%B4%E8%BD%84%E5%B8%82%E5%AE%9A%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F%E5%AF%BA%E5%BB%9F_Venation_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E8%89%8B%E8%88%BA%E9%BE%8D%E5%B1%B1%E5%AF%BA_%E8%87%BA%E5%8C%97%E5%B8%82_%E7%9B%B4%E8%BD%84%E5%B8%82%E5%AE%9A%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F%E5%AF%BA%E5%BB%9F_Venation_2.JPG)

[Lungshan Temple of Manka](/wiki/Lungshan_Temple_of_Manka "Lungshan Temple of Manka") in Taipei

There are over 22 million people of Han Chinese ancestry in living in Taiwan.[[106]](#cite_note-110) At first, these migrants chose to settle in locations that bore a resemblance to the areas they had left behind in China, regardless of whether they arrived in the north or south of Taiwan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Hoklo](/wiki/Hoklo_people "Hoklo people") immigrants from [Quanzhou](/wiki/Quanzhou "Quanzhou") settled in coastal regions and those from [Zhangzhou](/wiki/Zhangzhou "Zhangzhou") tended to gather on inland plains, while the [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people") inhabited hilly areas.

Clashes and tensions between the two groups over land, water, ethno-racial,[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Han_Chinese#Dubious "Talk:Han Chinese")*] and cultural differences led to the relocation of some communities and over time, varying degrees of intermarriage and assimilation took place. In Taiwan, Han Chinese (including both the earlier Han Taiwanese settlers and the recent Chinese that arrived in Taiwan with Chiang Kai-shek in 1949) constitute over 95% of the population. They have also been a politically, culturally and economically dominant majority vis-à-vis the non-Han [indigenous Taiwanese peoples](/wiki/Taiwanese_indigenous_peoples "Taiwanese indigenous peoples").[[105]](#cite_note-Chua_2003_https://archive.org/details/worldonfirehowex00chua_0/page/178_178-109)[[102]](#cite_note-Chua_2000_328-106)

### Southeast Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Southeast Asia")]

Main article: [Overseas Chinese § Southeast Asia](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese#Southeast_Asia "Overseas Chinese")

Nearly 30 to 40 million people of Han Chinese descent live in Southeast Asia.[[107]](#cite_note-111) According to a [population genetic](/wiki/Population_genetic "Population genetic") study, [Singapore](/wiki/Chinese_Singaporeans "Chinese Singaporeans") is "the country with the biggest proportion of Han Chinese" in Southeast Asia.[[108]](#cite_note-Ychrom_HanSingaporeans-112) Singapore is the only nation in the world where Overseas Chinese constitute a majority of the population and remain the country's cultural, economic and politically dominant arbiters vis-à-vis their non-Han minority counterparts.[[105]](#cite_note-Chua_2003_https://archive.org/details/worldonfirehowex00chua_0/page/178_178-109)[[109]](#cite_note-Vatikiotis_February_12th_1998-113)[[102]](#cite_note-Chua_2000_328-106) Up until the past few decades, overseas Han communities originated predominantly from areas in Eastern and Southeastern China (mainly from the provinces of [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") and [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), and to a lesser extent, [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"), [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") and [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang")).[[108]](#cite_note-Ychrom_HanSingaporeans-112)

### Others

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Others")]

Main article: [Overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese")

There are 60 million Overseas Chinese people worldwide.[[110]](#cite_note-114)[[111]](#cite_note-115)[[112]](#cite_note-116) Overseas Han Chinese have settled in numerous countries across the globe, particularly within the Western World where nearly 4 million people of Han Chinese descent live in the United States (about 1.5% of the population),[[113]](#cite_note-usasurveys2017-117) over 1 million in Australia (5.6%)[[15]](#cite_note-2016Aus-15)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] and about 1.5 million in Canada (5.1%),[[114]](#cite_note-2016Census-118)[[115]](#cite_note-www12.statcan.gc.ca-119)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] nearly 231,000 in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") (4.9%),[[116]](#cite_note-120)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] and as many as 750,000 in Sub-Saharan Africa.[[117]](#cite_note-121)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of China](/wiki/History_of_China "History of China")

The Han Chinese people have had a substantial impact on the history of China, being considered the ethnic majority of the region for most of its history. The prevailing historical narrative of China is often told as the transference of power through [dynasties](/wiki/Dynasties_of_China "Dynasties of China"), periods during which it has seen [cycles](/wiki/Dynastic_cycle "Dynastic cycle") of expansion, contraction, unity, and fragmentation. During this lengthy imperial period of dynastic rule, the Han people, much like the region itself, have seen periods of both global power[[118]](#cite_note-122)[[119]](#cite_note-123) and suppression,[[120]](#cite_note-124)[[121]](#cite_note-125) of strife and peace, of influence and isolation, and of unity and division. The Han Chinese have often been historically attributed to holding dominant positions of governance throughout this dynastic period of Chinese history[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], though in notable periods the dynastic rule has been [held by non-Han ethnic minorities](/wiki/Conquest_dynasty "Conquest dynasty") as well. Examples include the [Khitan](/wiki/Khitan_people "Khitan people")-lead [Liao dynasty](/wiki/Liao_dynasty "Liao dynasty")[[122]](#cite_note-:2-126) (916–1125) [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire")-lead [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty")[[122]](#cite_note-:2-126) (1271–1368), and the [Jurchen](/wiki/Jurchen_people "Jurchen people")-lead [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(1115%E2%80%931234) "Jin dynasty (1115–1234)")[[122]](#cite_note-:2-126) (1115–1234) and [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty")[[123]](#cite_note-127) (1644–1912; initially the "[later Jin](/wiki/Later_Jin_(1616%E2%80%931636) "Later Jin (1616–1636)")", 1616–1636[[124]](#cite_note-128)).

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

Main articles: [Huaxia](/wiki/Huaxia "Huaxia"), [Peopling of China](/wiki/Peopling_of_China "Peopling of China"), and [Yanhuang](/wiki/Yanhuang "Yanhuang")

[![Yellow Emperor](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Portraits_of_Famous_Men_-_Yellow_Emperor_%28Huangdi%29.jpg/250px-Portraits_of_Famous_Men_-_Yellow_Emperor_%28Huangdi%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portraits_of_Famous_Men_-_Yellow_Emperor_(Huangdi).jpg)

[![Yan Emperor](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Xu_Jetian_-_Shennong.jpg/250px-Xu_Jetian_-_Shennong.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xu_Jetian_-_Shennong.jpg)

Han Chinese refer to themselves as "[Descendants of Yan and Huang](/wiki/Yan_Huang_Zisun "Yan Huang Zisun")"

The prehistory of the Han Chinese is closely intertwined with both archaeology, biology, historical textual records, and mythology. The ethnic stock to which the Han Chinese originally trace their ancestry from were confederations of late [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") and early [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") agricultural tribes known as the [Huaxia](/wiki/Huaxia "Huaxia") that lived along the [Guanzhong](/wiki/Guanzhong "Guanzhong") and [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") basins in northern China.[[125]](#cite_note-PV-129)[[126]](#cite_note-130)[[127]](#cite_note-131)[[128]](#cite_note-132)[[129]](#cite_note-Guo_2016_66–67-133)[[130]](#cite_note-134)[[131]](#cite_note-135)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] In addition, numerous [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Chinese_history#List_of_ethnic_groups "Ethnic groups in Chinese history") were assimilated and absorbed by the Han Chinese at various points in China's history.[[129]](#cite_note-Guo_2016_66–67-133)[[132]](#cite_note-136)[[125]](#cite_note-PV-129) Like many modern ethnic groups, the ethnogenesis of Han Chinese was a lengthy process that involved the expansion of the [successive Chinese dynasties](/wiki/Dynasties_of_China "Dynasties of China") and their assimilation of [various non-Han ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Chinese_history#List_of_ethnic_groups "Ethnic groups in Chinese history").[[133]](#cite_note-137)[[134]](#cite_note-138)[[135]](#cite_note-139)[[136]](#cite_note-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Xuanyuan_Temple_in_Yan%27an%2C_Shaanxi.jpg/250px-Xuanyuan_Temple_in_Yan%27an%2C_Shaanxi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xuanyuan_Temple_in_Yan%27an,_Shaanxi.jpg)

[Mausoleum of the Yellow Emperor](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_the_Yellow_Emperor "Mausoleum of the Yellow Emperor") (*Huangdi*) in Shaanxi

During the [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou "Western Zhou") and Han dynasties, Han Chinese writers established genealogical lineages by drawing from legendary materials originating from the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty"),[[137]](#cite_note-141) while the Han dynasty historian [Sima Qian](/wiki/Sima_Qian "Sima Qian")'s *[Records of the Grand Historian](/wiki/Records_of_the_Grand_Historian "Records of the Grand Historian")* places the reign of the [Yellow Emperor](/wiki/Yellow_Emperor "Yellow Emperor"), the legendary leader of Youxiong tribes (有熊氏), at the beginning of Chinese history. The Yellow Emperor is traditionally credited to have united with the neighbouring [Shennong](/wiki/Shennong "Shennong") tribes after defeating their leader, the [Yan Emperor](/wiki/Yan_Emperor "Yan Emperor"), at the [Battle of Banquan](/wiki/Battle_of_Banquan "Battle of Banquan"). The newly merged [Yanhuang](/wiki/Yanhuang "Yanhuang") tribes then combined forces to defeat their common enemy from the east, [Chiyou](/wiki/Chiyou "Chiyou") of the [Jiuli tribes](/wiki/Jiuli_tribes "Jiuli tribes"), at the [Battle of Zhuolu](/wiki/Battle_of_Zhuolu "Battle of Zhuolu") and established their cultural dominance in the [Central Plain](/wiki/Central_Plain_(China) "Central Plain (China)") region. To this day, modern Han Chinese refer themselves as "[Descendants of Yan and Huang](/wiki/Yan_Huang_Zisun "Yan Huang Zisun")".

Although study of this period of history is complicated by the absence of contemporary records, the discovery of [archaeological sites](/wiki/Archaeological_site "Archaeological site") has enabled a succession of [Neolithic cultures](/wiki/List_of_Neolithic_cultures_of_China "List of Neolithic cultures of China") to be identified along the Yellow River. Along the middle and lower reaches of the Yellow River were the [Cishan culture](/wiki/Cishan_culture "Cishan culture") (c. 6500–5000 BCE), the [Yangshao culture](/wiki/Yangshao_culture "Yangshao culture") (c. 5000–3000 BCE), the [Longshan culture](/wiki/Longshan_culture "Longshan culture") (c. 3000–2000 BCE) and the [Erlitou culture](/wiki/Erlitou_culture "Erlitou culture") (c. 1900–1500 BCE). These cultures are believed to be related to the origins of the [Sino-Tibetan languages](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_languages "Sino-Tibetan languages") and later the [Sinitic languages](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages").[[138]](#cite_note-142)[[139]](#cite_note-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144)[[141]](#cite_note-145)[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESagartJacquesLaiRyder201910319–10320-146) They were the foundation for the formation of [Old Chinese](/wiki/Old_Chinese "Old Chinese") and the founding of the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty"), China's first confirmed dynasty.

* Neolithic forebears of Sino-Tibetan and Chinese-speaking peoples

* [![Yangshao culture pottery (5000–3000 BC)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Yangshao_Culture_Pottery_28.jpg/250px-Yangshao_Culture_Pottery_28.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yangshao_Culture_Pottery_28.jpg "Yangshao culture pottery (5000–3000 BC)")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Early history")]

Main article: [Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors](/wiki/Three_Sovereigns_and_Five_Emperors "Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors")

Early ancient Chinese history is largely legendary, consisting of mythical tales intertwined with sporadic annals written centuries to millennia later. Sima Qian's *Records of the Grand Historian* recorded a period following the Battle of Zhuolu, during the reign of successive generations of confederate overlords ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 共主) known as the [Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors](/wiki/Three_Sovereigns_and_Five_Emperors "Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors") (c. 2852–2070 BCE), who, allegedly, were elected to power among the tribes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This is a period for which scant reliable archaeological evidence exists – these sovereigns are largely regarded as [cultural heroes](/wiki/Cultural_hero "Cultural hero").

#### Xia dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Xia dynasty")]

Main article: [Xia dynasty](/wiki/Xia_dynasty "Xia dynasty")

Though modernly agreed to be mostly a product of legends and folklore, the first dynasty to be described in Chinese historical records is the Xia dynasty (c. 2070–1600 BCE).[[e]](#cite_note-Xia-147)[[143]](#cite_note-148) established by [Yu the Great](/wiki/Yu_the_Great "Yu the Great") after [Emperor Shun](/wiki/Emperor_Shun "Emperor Shun") abdicated leadership to reward Yu's work in taming the [Great Flood](/wiki/Great_Flood_(China) "Great Flood (China)"). In traditional narrative, this is primarily where the ethnic Han originate from. In myth, Yu's son, [Qi](/wiki/Qi_of_Xia "Qi of Xia"), managed to not only install himself as the next ruler, but also dictated his sons as heirs by default. This would have made the Xia dynasty to be the first civilization in China to be ruled by [genealogical succession](/wiki/Order_of_succession "Order of succession"). The civilizational prosperity of the Xia dynasty at this time is thought to have given rise to the name "Huaxia", a term that was used ubiquitously throughout history to define the Chinese nation.[[144]](#cite_note-149)

Conclusive archaeological evidence predating the 16th century BCE is, however, rarely available. Recent efforts of the [Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project](/wiki/Xia%E2%80%93Shang%E2%80%93Zhou_Chronology_Project "Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project") drew the connection between the [Erlitou culture](/wiki/Erlitou_culture "Erlitou culture") and the Xia dynasty, but scholars found this connection tenuous.[[145]](#cite_note-150) By exention, earliest writing by the Han Chinese are found in the period of the Shang dynasty.

#### Shang dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Shang dynasty")]

Main article: [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty")

The Xia dynasty was overthrown after the [Battle of Mingtiao](/wiki/Battle_of_Mingtiao "Battle of Mingtiao"), around 1600 BCE, by [Cheng Tang](/wiki/Tang_of_Shang "Tang of Shang"), who established the Shang dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE). The earliest archaeological examples of Chinese writing date back to this period – from characters inscribed on [oracle bones](/wiki/Oracle_bone "Oracle bone") used for divination – but the well-developed characters hint at a much earlier origin of writing in China.

During the Shang dynasty, people of the [Wu area](/wiki/Wu_(region) "Wu (region)") in the [Yangtze River Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_River_Delta "Yangtze River Delta") were considered a different tribe, and described as being scantily dressed, tattooed and speaking a distinct language. Later, [Taibo](/wiki/Taibo_of_Wu "Taibo of Wu"), elder uncle of [Ji Chang](/wiki/King_Wen_of_Zhou "King Wen of Zhou") – on realising that his younger brother, Jili, was wiser and deserved to inherit the throne – fled to Wu[[146]](#cite_note-chinaknowledge.de-151) and settled there. Three generations later, [King Wu](/wiki/King_Wu_of_Zhou "King Wu of Zhou") of the Zhou dynasty defeated [King Zhou](/wiki/King_Zhou_of_Shang "King Zhou of Shang") (the last Shang king), and [enfeoffed](/wiki/Feoffment "Feoffment") the descendants of Taibo in Wu[[146]](#cite_note-chinaknowledge.de-151) – mirroring the later history of [Nanyue](/wiki/Nanyue "Nanyue"), where a Chinese king and his soldiers ruled a non-Han population and mixed with locals, who were [sinicized](/wiki/Sinicization "Sinicization") over time.

#### Zhou dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Zhou dynasty")]

Main article: [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty")

After the [Battle of Muye](/wiki/Battle_of_Muye "Battle of Muye"), the Shang dynasty was overthrown by [Zhou](/wiki/Predynastic_Zhou "Predynastic Zhou") (led by [Ji Fa](/wiki/Ji_Fa "Ji Fa")), which had emerged as a western state along the [Wei River](/wiki/Wei_River "Wei River") in the 2nd millennium BCE. The [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") shared the language and culture of the Shang people, and extended their reach to encompass much of the area north of the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River").[[147]](#cite_note-China_The_Zhou_Period-152) Through conquest and colonization, much of this area came under the influence of sinicization and this culture extended south. However, the power of the Zhou kings fragmented not long afterwards, and many autonomous vassal states emerged. This dynasty is traditionally divided into two eras – the Western Zhou (1046–771 BCE) and the [Eastern Zhou](/wiki/Eastern_Zhou "Eastern Zhou") (770–256 BCE) – with the latter further divided into the [Spring and Autumn](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period") (770–476 BCE) and the [Warring States](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") (476–221 BCE) periods. It was a period of significant cultural and philosophical diversification (known as the [Hundred Schools of Thought](/wiki/Hundred_Schools_of_Thought "Hundred Schools of Thought")) and [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") and [Legalism](/wiki/Legalism_(Chinese_philosophy) "Legalism (Chinese philosophy)") are among the most important surviving philosophies from this era.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Imperial history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Imperial history")]

#### Qin dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Qin dynasty")]

Main article: [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty")

The chaotic Warring States period of the Eastern Zhou dynasty came to an end with the unification of China by the western state of [Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)") after its [conquest of all other rival states](/wiki/Qin%27s_wars_of_unification "Qin's wars of unification")[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] under King [Ying Zheng](/wiki/Ying_Zheng "Ying Zheng"). King Zheng then gave himself a new title "[First Emperor of Qin](/wiki/First_Emperor_of_Qin "First Emperor of Qin")" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 秦始皇帝; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Qín Shǐ Huángdì*), setting the precedent for the next two millennia. To consolidate administrative control over the newly conquered parts of the country, the First Emperor decreed a nationwide standardization of currency, writing scripts and measurement units, to unify the country economically and culturally. He also ordered large-scale infrastructure projects such as the [Great Wall](/wiki/Great_Wall_of_China "Great Wall of China"), the [Lingqu Canal](/wiki/Lingqu_Canal "Lingqu Canal") and the Qin road system to militarily fortify the frontiers. In effect, he established a centralized bureaucratic state to replace the old feudal confederation system of preceding dynasties, making Qin the first [imperial dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") in Chinese history.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This dynasty, sometimes phonetically spelt as the "Ch'in dynasty", has been proposed in the 17th century by [Martino Martini](/wiki/Martino_Martini "Martino Martini") and supported by later scholars such as [Paul Pelliot](/wiki/Paul_Pelliot "Paul Pelliot") and [Berthold Laufer](/wiki/Berthold_Laufer "Berthold Laufer") to be the etymological origin of the modern English word "China".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Han dynasty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Han dynasty")]

Main article: [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/China.Terracotta_statues007.jpg/250px-China.Terracotta_statues007.jpg)](/wiki/File:China.Terracotta_statues007.jpg)

A female servant and male advisor dressed in [silk robes](/wiki/Han_Chinese_clothing "Han Chinese clothing"), ceramic figurines from the Western Han era

The reign of the first imperial dynasty was short-lived. Due to the First Emperor's autocratic rule and his massive labor projects, which fomented rebellion among his population, the Qin dynasty fell into chaos soon after his death. Under the corrupt rule of his son and successor [Huhai](/wiki/Huhai "Huhai"), the Qin dynasty collapsed a mere three years later. The Han dynasty (206 BC–220 CE) then emerged from the ensuing [civil wars](/wiki/Chu-Han_contention "Chu-Han contention") and succeeded in establishing a much longer-lasting dynasty. It continued many of the institutions created by the Qin dynasty, but adopted a more moderate rule. Under the Han dynasty, art and culture flourished, while the Han Empire [expanded militarily in all directions](/wiki/History_of_the_Han_dynasty "History of the Han dynasty"). Many Chinese scholars such as [Ho Ping-ti](/wiki/Ho_Ping-ti "Ho Ping-ti") believe that the concept ([ethnogenesis](/wiki/Ethnogenesis "Ethnogenesis")) of Han ethnicity, although being ancient, was formally entrenched in the Han dynasty.[[148]](#cite_note-153) The Han dynasty is considered one of the [golden ages](/wiki/Golden_age "Golden age") of Chinese history, with the modern Han Chinese people taking their ethnic name from this dynasty and the Chinese script being referred to as "[Han characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters")".[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchaefer2008279-154)

#### Three Kingdoms to Jin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Three Kingdoms to Jin")]

Main articles: [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms"), [Sixteen Kingdoms](/wiki/Sixteen_Kingdoms "Sixteen Kingdoms"), [Northern and Southern dynasties](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_dynasties "Northern and Southern dynasties"), [Sui dynasty](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty"), and [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty")

The fall of the Han dynasty was followed by an age of fragmentation and several centuries of disunity amid warfare among rival kingdoms. There was a brief period of prosperity under the native Han Chinese dynasty known as the [Jin](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)") (266–420 AD), although protracted struggles within the ruling house of Sima (司馬) sparked off a protracted period of fragmentation, rebellion by immigrant tribes that served as slaves and indentured servants, and extended non-native rule.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Non-Han rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Non-Han rule")]
