Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/%E6%99%8B%E5%A4%AA%E4%BF%9D%E5%BB%BA%E6%98%8C%E5%85%AC%E8%B0%A2%E5%AE%89.jpg/250px-%E6%99%8B%E5%A4%AA%E4%BF%9D%E5%BB%BA%E6%98%8C%E5%85%AC%E8%B0%A2%E5%AE%89.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%99%8B%E5%A4%AA%E4%BF%9D%E5%BB%BA%E6%98%8C%E5%85%AC%E8%B0%A2%E5%AE%89.jpg)

[Xie An](/wiki/Xie_An "Xie An"), who led the [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)") army defeated the [Di](/wiki/Di_(Five_Barbarians) "Di (Five Barbarians)") (one of the [Five Barbarians](/wiki/Five_Barbarians "Five Barbarians")) ruled [Former Qin](/wiki/Former_Qin "Former Qin") army in the [Battle of Feishui](/wiki/Battle_of_Feishui "Battle of Feishui") was honoured in the [Table of Peerless Heroes](/wiki/Wu_Shuang_Pu "Wu Shuang Pu").

During this time, areas of northern China were overrun by various [non-Han nomadic peoples](/wiki/Five_Barbarians "Five Barbarians"), which came to establish kingdoms of their own, the most successful of which was the [Northern Wei](/wiki/Northern_Wei "Northern Wei") established by the [Xianbei](/wiki/Xianbei "Xianbei").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] From this period, the native population of China proper was referred to as Hanren, or the "People of Han" to distinguish them from the nomads from the steppe. Warfare and invasion led to one of the first great migrations of Han populations in history, as they fled south to the [Yangzi](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") and beyond, shifting the Chinese demographic center and speeding up sinicization of the far south. At the same time, most of the nomads in northern China came to be sinicized as they ruled over large Chinese populations and adopted elements of their culture and administration. Of note, the Xianbei rulers of Northern Wei ordered a policy of systematic sinicization, [adopting Han surnames](/wiki/Change_of_Xianbei_names_to_Han_names "Change of Xianbei names to Han names"), institutions, and culture, so the Xianbei became Han Chinese.

#### Sui and Tang

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sui and Tang")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/China%2C_742.svg/250px-China%2C_742.svg.png)](/wiki/File:China,_742.svg)

Map of Tang Empire in 742, showing the [major provinces](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_Tang_dynasty "Administrative divisions of the Tang dynasty") of the empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Figures_in_a_cortege%2C_tomb_of_Li_Xian%2C_Tang_Dynasty.jpg/250px-Figures_in_a_cortege%2C_tomb_of_Li_Xian%2C_Tang_Dynasty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Figures_in_a_cortege,_tomb_of_Li_Xian,_Tang_Dynasty.jpg)

Cortege of Emperor Li Xian, Emperor of the Tang

> 敵可摧，旄頭滅，履胡之腸涉胡血。  
> 懸胡青天上，埋胡紫塞傍。  
> 胡無人，漢道昌。  
> The enemy can be crushed, their banners destroyed;  
>  We tread upon the entrails of the [Hu](/wiki/Hu_(people) "Hu (people)"), wade through their blood.  
> Hang the bodies of the Hu beneath the heavens, bury them beside the frontier.  
> No [Hu](/wiki/Hu_(people) "Hu (people)") will remain, and the Han will always prosper

[Li Bai](/wiki/Li_Bai "Li Bai") (701-762)

Han Chinese rule resumed during the Sui and Tang dynasties, led by the Han Chinese families of the Yang (杨) and Li (李) surnames respectively. Both the Sui and Tang dynasties are seen as high points of Han Chinese civilization. These dynasties both emphasized their aristocratic Han Chinese pedigree and enforced the restoration of Central Plains culture, even the founders of both dynasties had already intermarried with non-Han or partly-Han women from the Dugu and Yuwen families.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Sui (581–618) and Tang (618–907) dynasties saw continuing emigration from the Central Plains to the south-eastern coast of what is now China proper, including the provinces of [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), and [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"). This was especially true in the latter part of the Tang era and the Five Dynasties period that followed; the relative stability of the south coast made it an attractive destination for refugees fleeing continual warfare and turmoil in the north.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Song to Qing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Song to Qing")]

Main articles: [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), and [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%8B_%C2%B7_%E6%96%97%E6%B5%86%E5%9B%BE_Tea_Fighting_Southern_Song_dynasty.png/250px-%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%8B_%C2%B7_%E6%96%97%E6%B5%86%E5%9B%BE_Tea_Fighting_Southern_Song_dynasty.png)](/wiki/File:%E5%8D%97%E5%AE%8B_%C2%B7_%E6%96%97%E6%B5%86%E5%9B%BE_Tea_Fighting_Southern_Song_dynasty.png)

Tea competition in Song China

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/%E5%8F%B2%E5%8F%AF%E6%B3%95%E5%83%8F.jpg/250px-%E5%8F%B2%E5%8F%AF%E6%B3%95%E5%83%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%8F%B2%E5%8F%AF%E6%B3%95%E5%83%8F.jpg)

[Shi Kefa](/wiki/Shi_Kefa "Shi Kefa"), the [Minister of War](/wiki/Three_Departments_and_Six_Ministries "Three Departments and Six Ministries") of [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), was famous for his tenacious defence in Yangzhou against the [Manchu](/wiki/Manchu "Manchu") ([Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty")) invasion followed by the [Yangzhou massacre](/wiki/Yangzhou_massacre "Yangzhou massacre") commanded by the Manchu army.

The next few centuries saw successive invasions of Han and non-Han peoples from the north. In 1279, the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") conquered all of China, becoming the first non-Han ethnic group to do so, and established the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"). [Emigration](/wiki/Emigration "Emigration"), seen as disloyal to ancestors and ancestral land, was banned by the Song and Yuan dynasties.[[150]](#cite_note-155)

[Zhu Yuanzhang](/wiki/Hongwu_Emperor "Hongwu Emperor"), who had a [Han-centered concept of China](/wiki/Sinocentrism "Sinocentrism"), and regarded expelling "[barbarians](/wiki/Hua%E2%80%93Yi_distinction "Hua–Yi distinction")" and restoring Han people's China as a mission, established the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") in 1368 after the [Red Turban Rebellions](/wiki/Red_Turban_Rebellions "Red Turban Rebellions"). During this period, China referred to the Ming Empire and to the Han people living in them, and non-Han communities were separated from China.[[151]](#cite_note-md-156)

Early [Manchu](/wiki/Manchu_people "Manchu people") rulers treated China as equivalent to both the Ming Empire and to the Han group.[[151]](#cite_note-md-156) In 1644, the Ming capital, [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), was captured by [Li Zicheng](/wiki/Li_Zicheng "Li Zicheng")'s peasant rebels and the [Chongzhen Emperor](/wiki/Chongzhen_Emperor "Chongzhen Emperor") committed suicide. The Manchus of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") then allied with former Ming general [Wu Sangui](/wiki/Wu_Sangui "Wu Sangui") and seized control of Beijing. Remnant Ming forces led by [Koxinga](/wiki/Koxinga "Koxinga") fled to [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") and established the [Kingdom of Tungning](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Tungning "Kingdom of Tungning"), which eventually capitulated to Qing forces in 1683. Taiwan, previously inhabited mostly by non-Han aborigines, was [sinicized](/wiki/Sinicization "Sinicization") during this period via large-scale migration accompanied by assimilation, despite efforts by the Manchus to prevent this, as they found it difficult to maintain control over the island. In 1681, the [Kangxi Emperor](/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor") ordered construction of the [Willow Palisade](/wiki/Willow_Palisade "Willow Palisade") to prevent Han Chinese migration to the three northeastern provinces, which nevertheless had harbored a significant Chinese population for centuries, especially in the southern [Liaodong](/wiki/Liaodong "Liaodong") area. The Manchus designated Jilin and Heilongjiang as the Manchu homeland, to which the Manchus could hypothetically escape and regroup if the Qing dynasty fell.[[152]](#cite_note-157) Because of increasing Russian territorial encroachment and annexation of neighboring territory, the Qing later reversed its policy and allowed the consolidation of a demographic Han majority in Northeast China. The [Taiping Rebellion](/wiki/Taiping_Rebellion "Taiping Rebellion") erupted in 1850 from the [anti-Manchu](/wiki/Anti-Qing_sentiment "Anti-Qing sentiment") sentiment of the Han Chinese, which killed at least twenty million people and made it [one of the bloodiest conflicts](/wiki/List_of_wars_by_death_toll "List of wars by death toll") in history.[[153]](#cite_note-hm-158) Late Qing revolutionary intellectual [Zou Rong](/wiki/Zou_Rong "Zou Rong") famously proclaimed that "China is the China of the Chinese. We compatriots should identify ourselves with the China of the Han Chinese".[[154]](#cite_note-ww-159)

### Republican history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Republican history")]

Main articles: [History of the Republic of China](/wiki/History_of_the_Republic_of_China "History of the Republic of China") and [History of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "History of the People's Republic of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/1912Jimingxiaoling.jpg/250px-1912Jimingxiaoling.jpg)](/wiki/File:1912Jimingxiaoling.jpg)

[Sun Yat-sen](/wiki/Sun_Yat-sen "Sun Yat-sen") (center) and other [ROC](/wiki/Republic_of_china "Republic of china") officials commemorated [Hongwu Emperor](/wiki/Hongwu_Emperor "Hongwu Emperor") in [Xiao Mausoleum](/wiki/Xiao_Mausoleum "Xiao Mausoleum"), February 1912

The [Han nationalist](/wiki/Han_nationalism "Han nationalism") revolutionary [Sun Yat-sen](/wiki/Sun_Yat-sen "Sun Yat-sen") made [Han Chinese superiority](/wiki/Han_chauvinism "Han chauvinism") a basic tenet of the [1911 Revolution](/wiki/1911_Revolution "1911 Revolution").[[155]](#cite_note-so-160) In Sun's revolutionary philosophical view, Han identity is exclusively possessed by the so-called civilized Hua Xia people who originated from the Central Plains, and were also the former subjects of the [Celestial empire](/wiki/Celestial_Empire "Celestial Empire") and evangelists of Confucianism.[[154]](#cite_note-ww-159) Restoring Chinese rule to the Han majority was one of the motivations for supporters of the 1911 Revolution to overthrow the Manchu-led [Qing dynasty in 1912](/wiki/Imperial_Edict_of_the_Abdication_of_the_Qing_Emperor "Imperial Edict of the Abdication of the Qing Emperor"), which led to the establishment of the Han-dominated [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)").[[156]](#cite_note-161) After the establishment of the republic, Sun went to offer sacrifices in [Hongwu Emperor](/wiki/Hongwu_Emperor "Hongwu Emperor")'s [Xiao Mausoleum](/wiki/Xiao_Mausoleum "Xiao Mausoleum"):

> 我高皇帝，應時崛起，廓清中土，日月重明，河山再造，光復大義，昭示來茲。  
> 不幸季世俶擾，國力罷疲，滿清乘間，入據中夏。嗟我邦人，諸父兄弟，迭起迭踣，至於二百六十有八年。  
> ...
>   
> 武漢軍興，建立民國。義聲所播，天下響應，越八十有七日，旣光復十有七省，國民公議，立臨時政府於南京。
>
> ---
>
> Our [Emperor Gaozu](/wiki/Hongwu_Emperor "Hongwu Emperor"), rose in due time to pacify the [Central Earth](/wiki/Zhongyuan "Zhongyuan"), restoring clarity to sun and moon, rebuilding rivers and mountains, reviving the great righteousness and proclaiming it to posterity.  
>  Alas, in the troubled age at the end of the dynasty, the nation's strength was exhausted, and the [Manchus](/wiki/Manchus "Manchus") took the opportunity to invade and occupy China.   
> Alas, our compatriots—forefathers, elder and younger brothers—rose and fell one after another, for as long as 268 years.  
> ...
>   
> When the [Revolt began in Wuhan](/wiki/Wuchang_Uprising "Wuchang Uprising"), the Republic was established. Wherever the call of righteousness spread, the whole nation responded.   
> After just eighty-seven days, [seventeen provinces](/wiki/China_proper "China proper") had been restored. By the will of the people, a provisional government was established in Nanjing through public consensus.

[Sun Yat-sen](/wiki/Sun_Yat-sen "Sun Yat-sen") (1866-1925)

[Chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") and his [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") founded in 1949 was critical of Han chauvinism.[[153]](#cite_note-hm-158) In the latter half of the 20th century, official policy of [communist](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") China marked Han chauvinism as [anti-Marxist](/wiki/Criticism_of_Marxism "Criticism of Marxism").[[155]](#cite_note-so-160) Today, the tension between the dominant Han Chinese majority and [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities_in_China "Ethnic minorities in China") remains contentious, as the deterioration in ethnic relations has compounded by [China's contemporary ethnic policies in favor of ethnic minorities](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_China "Affirmative action in China") since its founding.[[154]](#cite_note-ww-159) [Han chauvinism](/wiki/Han_chauvinism "Han chauvinism") has been gaining mainstream popularity throughout China since the 2000s, attributed to discontent toward these ethnic policies instituted by the Chinese government.[[157]](#cite_note-凭栏观史-162)[[158]](#cite_note-皇汉史观：今天我们如何定义中国？-163) The contemporary dissatisfaction and discord between the dominant Han Chinese mainstream and its non-Han minorities has led to the Chinese government scaling back on [preferential treatment](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_China "Affirmative action in China") for ethnic minorities under the [general secretaryship of Xi Jinping](/wiki/General_secretaryship_of_Xi_Jinping "General secretaryship of Xi Jinping").[[159]](#cite_note-164)

## Culture and society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Culture and society")]

Main article: [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture")

Chinese civilization is one of the world's oldest and most complex[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] [civilizations](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization"), whose culture dates back thousands of years. Overseas Han Chinese maintain cultural affinities to Chinese territories outside of their host locale through [ancestor worship](/wiki/Ancestor_worship "Ancestor worship") and [clan](/wiki/Chinese_clan "Chinese clan") associations, which often identify famous figures from Chinese history or myth as ancestors of current members.[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165) Such patriarchs include the Yellow Emperor and the [Yan Emperor](/wiki/Yan_Emperor "Yan Emperor"), who according to legend lived thousands of years ago and gave Han people the sobriquet "*[Descendants of Yan and Huang Emperor](/wiki/Descendants_of_Yan_and_Huang_Emperor "Descendants of Yan and Huang Emperor")*" (炎黃子孫, 炎黄子孙), a phrase which has reverberative connotations in a divisive political climate, as in that of major contentions between [China and Taiwan](/wiki/Cross-Strait_relations "Cross-Strait relations").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Along_the_River_During_the_Qingming_Festival_%28detail_of_original%29.jpg/250px-Along_the_River_During_the_Qingming_Festival_%28detail_of_original%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Along_the_River_During_the_Qingming_Festival_(detail_of_original).jpg)

[Zhang Zeduan](/wiki/Zhang_Zeduan "Zhang Zeduan")'s painting *[Along the River During the Qingming Festival](/wiki/Along_the_River_During_the_Qingming_Festival "Along the River During the Qingming Festival")* captures the daily life of people from the Song dynasty at the capital, Bianjing, today's [Kaifeng](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng").

The Han Chinese also share a distinct set of cultural practices, traditions, and beliefs that have evolved over centuries. Traditional Han customs, art, dietary habits, literature, religious beliefs, and value systems have not only deeply influenced Han culture itself, but also the cultures of its East Asian neighbors as well.[[161]](#cite_note-166)[[162]](#cite_note-Reischauer-167)[[163]](#cite_note-168)[[164]](#cite_note-169)[[165]](#cite_note-Amy_Chua,_Jed_Rubenfeld_2014_121-170)[[166]](#cite_note-Kang_2012_33–34-171)[[167]](#cite_note-172)[[168]](#cite_note-2000years-173)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] [Chinese art](/wiki/Chinese_art "Chinese art"), [Chinese architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture "Chinese architecture"), [Chinese cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine"), [Chinese dance](/wiki/Dance_in_China "Dance in China"), [Chinese fashion](/wiki/Chinese_clothing "Chinese clothing"), [Chinese festivals](/wiki/List_of_observances_set_by_the_Chinese_calendar "List of observances set by the Chinese calendar"), [Chinese holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_China "Public holidays in China"), [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), [Chinese literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature"), [Chinese music](/wiki/Music_of_China "Music of China"), [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology"), [Chinese numerology](/wiki/Chinese_numerology "Chinese numerology"), [Chinese philosophy](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy"), and [Chinese theatre](/wiki/Theatre_in_China "Theatre in China") all have undergone thousands of years of development and growth, while numerous Chinese sites, such as the [Great Wall](/wiki/Great_Wall_of_China "Great Wall of China") and the [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army"), are [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Since this program was launched in 2001, aspects of Chinese culture have been listed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as [Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Masterpieces_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "Masterpieces of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity"). Throughout the [history of China](/wiki/History_of_China "History of China"), Chinese culture has been heavily influenced by Confucianism. Credited with shaping much of Chinese philosophical thought, Confucianism was the official state philosophical doctrine throughout most of [Imperial China](/wiki/Imperial_Chinese "Imperial Chinese")'s history, institutionalizing values such as [filial piety](/wiki/Filial_piety "Filial piety"), which implied the performance of certain [shared rituals](/wiki/Li_(Confucian) "Li (Confucian)"). Thus, villagers lavished on [funeral](/wiki/Chinese_funeral_rituals "Chinese funeral rituals") and [wedding](/wiki/Chinese_wedding "Chinese wedding") ceremonies that imitated the Confucian standards of the Emperors.[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165) Educational achievement and academic success gained through years of arduous study and mastery of [classical Confucian texts](/wiki/Confucian_classics "Confucian classics") was an imperative duty for defending and protecting one's [family honor](/wiki/Family_honor#East_Asia "Family honor") while also providing the primary qualifying basis criterion for entry among ambitious individuals who sought to hold high ranking and influential [government positions](/wiki/Mandarin_(bureaucrat) "Mandarin (bureaucrat)") of distinguished authority, importance, responsibility, and power within the upper echelons of the [imperial bureaucracy](/wiki/Civil_service#China "Civil service").[[169]](#cite_note-McKnight1983-174)[[170]](#cite_note-TC-175)[[171]](#cite_note-176) But even among successful test takers and [degree-holders](/wiki/Jinshi "Jinshi") who did not enter the imperial bureaucracy or who left it opting out to pursue other careers experienced significant improvements with respect to their credibility, pedigree, respectability, social status, and societal influence, resulting in a considerable amelioration with regards to the [esteem](/wiki/Respect#Chinese_culture "Respect"), [glory](/wiki/Glory_(honor) "Glory (honor)"), [honor](/wiki/Family_honor#East_Asia "Family honor"), [prestige](/wiki/Reputation "Reputation"), and [recognition](/wiki/Recognition_(sociology) "Recognition (sociology)") that they brought and garnered to their families, social circles, and the localities that they hailed from. This elevation in their social standing, respectability, and pedigree was greatly augmented both within their own family circles, as well as among their neighbors and peers compared with the regular levels of recognition that they would have typically enjoyed had they only chosen to remain as mere commoners back in their ancestral regions. Yet even such a dynamic social phenomenon has greatly influenced Han society, leading to the homogenization of the Han populace. Additionally, it has played a crucial role in the formation of a socially cohesive and distinct shared Han culture as well as the overall growth and integration of Han society. This development has been facilitated by various extraneous factors, including periods of rapid urbanization and sprouts of geographically extensive yet interconnected commodity markets.[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Language")]

Main article: [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language")

See also: [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")

Han Chinese speak various forms of the Chinese language that are descended from a common early language;[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165) one of the names of the language groups is *Hanyu* (simplified Chinese: [汉语](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B1%89%E8%AF%AD "wikt:汉语"); traditional Chinese: [漢語](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BC%A2%E8%AA%9E "wikt:漢語")), literally the "Han language". Similarly, [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters"), used to write the language, are called *Hanzi* (simplified Chinese: [汉字](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B1%89%E5%AD%97 "wikt:汉字"); traditional Chinese: [漢字](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BC%A2%E5%AD%97 "wikt:漢字")) or "Han characters".

In the Qing era, more than two-thirds of the Han Chinese population used a variant of [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") as their native tongue.[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165) However, there was a larger variety of languages in certain areas of Southeast China, "in an arc extending roughly from [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") through [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") and into [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi")."[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165) Since the Qin dynasty, which standardized the various forms of writing that existed in China, a standard [literary Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") had emerged with vocabulary and grammar that was significantly different from the various [forms of spoken Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"). A simplified and elaborated version of this written standard was used in business contracts, notes for [Chinese opera](/wiki/Chinese_opera "Chinese opera"), ritual texts for [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") and other daily documents for educated people.[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165)

During the early 20th century, [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese") based on Mandarin dialects, which had been developing for several centuries, was standardized and adopted to replace literary Chinese. While written vernacular forms of other varieties of Chinese exist, such as [written Cantonese](/wiki/Written_Cantonese "Written Cantonese"), written Chinese based on Mandarin is widely understood by speakers of all varieties and has taken up the dominant position among written forms, formerly occupied by literary Chinese. Thus, although residents of different regions would not necessarily understand each other's speech, they generally share a common written language, Standard Written Chinese and Literary Chinese.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From the 1950s, [Simplified Chinese characters](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") were adopted in China and later in Singapore and Malaysia, while Chinese communities in Hong Kong, Macau, Taiwan and overseas countries continue to use [Traditional Chinese characters](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters").[[172]](#cite_note-177) Although significant differences exist between the two character sets, they are largely [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible").

### Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Names")]

Main articles: [Chinese name](/wiki/Chinese_name "Chinese name") and [List of common Chinese surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames")

Through China, the notion of [hundred surnames](/wiki/Baixing "Baixing") (百家姓) is a crucial identity point of the Han people.[[173]](#cite_note-Anth-178)

### Fashion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Fashion")]

Main articles: [Chinese clothing](/wiki/Chinese_clothing "Chinese clothing") and [Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Gu_Hongzhong%27s_Night_Revels%2C_Detail_1.jpg/250px-Gu_Hongzhong%27s_Night_Revels%2C_Detail_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gu_Hongzhong%27s_Night_Revels,_Detail_1.jpg)

A [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") Chinese painting *Night Revels of Han Xizai* showing scholars in scholar's robes and musicians dressed in a [Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu") variant; 12th-century remake of a 10th-century original by [Gu Hongzhong](/wiki/Gu_Hongzhong "Gu Hongzhong").

Han Chinese clothing has been shaped through its dynastic traditions as well as foreign influences.[[174]](#cite_note-179) Han Chinese clothing showcases the traditional fashion sensibilities of Chinese clothing traditions and forms one of the major cultural facets of Chinese civilization.[[175]](#cite_note-Brown,_2006_79-180) [Hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu") comprises all traditional clothing classifications of the Han Chinese with a recorded history of more than three millennia until the end of the Ming dynasty. During the Qing dynasty, Hanfu was mostly replaced by the Manchu style until the dynasty's fall in 1911, yet Han women continued to wear clothing from the Ming dynasty. Manchu and Han fashions of women's clothing coexisted during the Qing dynasty.[[176]](#cite_note-181)[[177]](#cite_note-Yang2004-182) Moreover, neither Taoist priests nor Buddhist monks were required to wear the queue by the Qing; they continued to wear their traditional hairstyles, completely shaved heads for Buddhist monks, and long hair in the traditional Chinese topknot for Taoist priests.[[178]](#cite_note-183)[[179]](#cite_note-Gerini1895-184) During the Republic of China period, fashion styles and forms of traditional Qing costumes gradually changed, influenced by fashion sensibilities from the Western World resulting modern Han Chinese wearing Western style clothing as a part of everyday dress.[[180]](#cite_note-185)[[175]](#cite_note-Brown,_2006_79-180)

Han Chinese clothing has continued to play an influential role within the realm of traditional East Asian fashion as both the Japanese [Kimono](/wiki/Kimono "Kimono") and the Korean [Hanbok](/wiki/Hanbok "Hanbok") were influenced by Han Chinese clothing designs.[[181]](#cite_note-186)[[182]](#cite_note-187)[[183]](#cite_note-188)[[184]](#cite_note-189)[[185]](#cite_note-Evenson-190)

### Family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Family")]

[Han Chinese families](/wiki/Chinese_kinship "Chinese kinship") throughout China have had certain traditionally prescribed roles, such as the family head (家長, *jiāzhǎng*), who represents the family to the outside world and the family manager (當家, *dāngjiā*), who is in charge of the revenues. Because farmland was commonly bought, sold or [mortgaged](/wiki/Mortgage "Mortgage"), families were run like enterprises, with set rules for the allocation (分家, *fēnjiā*) of pooled earnings and assets.[[160]](#cite_note-Cohen-165)

Han Chinese houses differ from place to place. In Beijing, the whole family traditionally lived together in a large rectangle-shaped house called a *[siheyuan](/wiki/Siheyuan "Siheyuan")*. Such houses had four rooms at the front – guest room, [kitchen](/wiki/Kitchen "Kitchen"), [lavatory](/wiki/Toilet_(room) "Toilet (room)") and [servants' quarters](/wiki/Servants%27_quarters "Servants' quarters"). Across large double doors was a wing for the elderly in the family. This wing consisted of three rooms: a central room where the four tablets – heaven, earth, ancestor and teacher – were worshipped and two rooms attached to the left and right, which were [bedrooms](/wiki/Bedrooms "Bedrooms") for the grandparents. The east wing of the house was inhabited by the eldest son and his family, while the west wing sheltered the second son and his family. Each wing had a [veranda](/wiki/Veranda "Veranda"); some had a "sunroom" made with surrounding fabric and supported by a wooden or [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo") frame. Every wing was also built around a central courtyard that was used for study, exercise or nature viewing.[[186]](#cite_note-191)

Ancestry and lineage are an important part of Han Chinese cultural practice and self-identity, and there have been strict naming conventions since the time of the Song dynasty that have been preserved until this day. Elaborate and detailed genealogies and family registers are maintained, and most lineage branches of all surname groups will maintain a hall containing the memorial tablets (also known as spirit tablets) of deceased family members in clan halls. Extended family groupings have been very important to the Han Chinese, and there are strict conventions as how one may refer to aunts, uncles, and cousins and the spouses of the same, depending on their birth order as well as whether these blood relatives share the same surname.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ancestral halls and academies, as well as tombs were of great import to the Chinese. Ancestral halls were used for the veneration or commemoration of ancestors and other large family events. Family members preferred to be buried near one another. Academies were also set up to benefit those of the same surname.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
