### Food

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Food")]

Main article: [Chinese cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine")

There is no one specific uniform [cuisine](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine") of the Han Chinese since the culinary traditions and food consumed varies from [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan")'s famously [spicy food](/wiki/Sichuan_cuisine "Sichuan cuisine") to Guangdong's [dim sum](/wiki/Dim_sum "Dim sum") and [fresh seafood](/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine#Seafood "Cantonese cuisine").[[187]](#cite_note-192) Analyses throughout the reaches of [Northern and Southern China](/wiki/Northern_and_southern_China "Northern and southern China") have revealed their main staple to be rice (more likely to consumed by southerners) as well as noodles and other wheat-based food items (which are more likely to be eaten by northerners).[[188]](#cite_note-eberhard-193) During China's Neolithic period, southwestern rice growers transitioned to millet from the northwest, when they could not find a suitable northwestern ecology – which was typically dry and cold – to sustain the generous yields of their staple as well as it did in other areas, such as along the eastern Chinese coast.[[189]](#cite_note-194)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main article: [Chinese literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/%E9%B2%81%E8%BF%85%E4%BA%94%E5%8D%81%E4%B8%89%E5%B2%81%E7%94%9F%E8%BE%B0.jpg/250px-%E9%B2%81%E8%BF%85%E4%BA%94%E5%8D%81%E4%B8%89%E5%B2%81%E7%94%9F%E8%BE%B0.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E9%B2%81%E8%BF%85%E4%BA%94%E5%8D%81%E4%B8%89%E5%B2%81%E7%94%9F%E8%BE%B0.jpg)

[Lu Xun](/wiki/Lu_Xun "Lu Xun") is one of the founding figures of modern Chinese literature.[[190]](#cite_note-195)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Jin_Yong%2C_July_2007.jpg/250px-Jin_Yong%2C_July_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jin_Yong,_July_2007.jpg)

Louis Cha, better known as the *[wuxia](/wiki/Wuxia "Wuxia")* writer [Jin Yong](/wiki/Jin_Yong "Jin Yong"), is one of the most popular Chinese writers of all time.[[191]](#cite_note-196)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Dufu.jpg/250px-Dufu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dufu.jpg)

[Du Fu](/wiki/Du_Fu "Du Fu") is often considered one of the greatest Chinese poets.[[192]](#cite_note-ebrey2006-197)

With a rich historical literary heritage spanning over three thousand years, the Han Chinese have continued to push the boundaries that have circumscribed the standards of literary excellence by showcasing an unwaveringly exceptional caliber and extensive wealth of literary accomplishments throughout the ages. The Han Chinese possess a vast catalogue of [classical literature](/wiki/Chinese_classic_texts "Chinese classic texts") that can be traced back as far as three millennia, with a body of literature encompassing significant early works such as the *[Classic of Poetry](/wiki/Shijing "Shijing")*, *[Analects of Confucius](/wiki/Analects_of_Confucius "Analects of Confucius")*, *[I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching")*, *[Tao Te Ching](/wiki/Tao_Te_Ching "Tao Te Ching")* and the *[Art of War](/wiki/The_Art_of_War "The Art of War")*. Canonical works of [Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"), Confucianism, and [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") alongside historical writings, philosophical works, treatises, poetry, drama, and fiction have been revered and immortalized as timeless cultural masterpieces within the vast expanse of Chinese literature. Historically, ambitious individuals who aspired to seek top [government positions](/wiki/Mandarin_(bureaucrat) "Mandarin (bureaucrat)") of distinguished authority, importance, and power were mandated to demonstrate their proficiency in the Confucian classics assessed through rigorous [examinations](/wiki/Imperial_examination "Imperial examination") in Imperial China.[[169]](#cite_note-McKnight1983-174)[[170]](#cite_note-TC-175) Such comprehensive examinations were not only employed as the prevailing universal standards to evaluate a candidate's ethical behavior and virtuous conduct, but were also deployed as a measure of academic aptitude that determined a candidate's caliber, credibility, and eligibility for such esteemed roles of great influence and responsibility, extending beyond their prevailing entrance as a gateway into the imperial bureaucracy. Han literature itself has a rich tradition dating back thousands of years, from the earliest recorded dynastic court archives to the mature vernacular fiction novels that arose during the Ming dynasty which were employed as a source of cultural pleasure to entertain the masses of literate Chinese. Some of the most important Han Chinese poets in the pre-modern era were [Li Bai](/wiki/Li_Bai "Li Bai"), [Du Fu](/wiki/Du_Fu "Du Fu") and [Su Dongpo](/wiki/Su_Shi "Su Shi"). The most esteemed and noteworthy novels of great literary significance in Chinese literature, otherwise known as the Four Great Classical Novels are: *[Dream of the Red Chamber](/wiki/Dream_of_the_Red_Chamber "Dream of the Red Chamber")*, *[Water Margin](/wiki/Water_Margin "Water Margin")*, *[Romance of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Romance_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Romance of the Three Kingdoms")* and *[Journey to the West](/wiki/Journey_to_the_West "Journey to the West")*.

Drawing upon their extensive literary heritage rooted in a historical legacy spanning over three thousand years, the Han Chinese have continued to demonstrate a uniformly high level of literary achievement throughout the modern era as the reputation of contemporary Chinese literature continues to be internationally recognized. Erudite literary scholars who are well-versed in Chinese literature continue to remain highly esteemed in contemporary Chinese society. [Liu Cixin](/wiki/Liu_Cixin "Liu Cixin")'s *[San Ti](/wiki/San_Ti "San Ti")* series won the [Hugo Award](/wiki/Hugo_Award "Hugo Award").[[193]](#cite_note-198) [Gao Xingjian](/wiki/Gao_Xingjian "Gao Xingjian") became the first Chinese novelist to receive the [Nobel Prize for Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 2000. In 2012, the novelist and short story writer [Mo Yan](/wiki/Mo_Yan "Mo Yan") also received the Nobel Prize in Literature. In 2015, children's writer [Cao Wenxuan](/wiki/Cao_Wenxuan "Cao Wenxuan") was bestowed with the [Hans Christian Andersen Award](/wiki/Hans_Christian_Andersen_Award "Hans Christian Andersen Award"), the first Chinese recipient of the esteemed international children's book prize.[[194]](#cite_note-199)

### Science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Science and technology")]

Main articles: [List of Chinese inventions](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_inventions "List of Chinese inventions") and [List of Chinese discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_discoveries "List of Chinese discoveries")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/%E5%BE%90%E5%85%89%E5%95%9F.jpg/250px-%E5%BE%90%E5%85%89%E5%95%9F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%BE%90%E5%85%89%E5%95%9F.jpg)

[Xu Guangqi](/wiki/Xu_Guangqi "Xu Guangqi"), was an agronomist, astronomer and mathematician. He wrote *[Complete Treatise on Agriculture](/wiki/Complete_Treatise_on_Agriculture "Complete Treatise on Agriculture")* and was one of the main editors of [*Chongzhen Calendar*](/wiki/Chongzhen_calendar "Chongzhen calendar").

The Han Chinese have made significant contributions to various fields in the advancement and progress of human civilization, including business and economy, culture and society, governance, and [science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_China "Science and technology in China"), both historically and in the modern era. They have also played a pivotal role in being at the forefront of shaping the evolutionary trajectory of Chinese civilization and significantly influenced the advancement of East Asian civilization in concurrence with the broader region of East Asia as a whole. The invention of paper, printing, the compass and gunpowder are celebrated in Chinese society as the [Four Great Inventions](/wiki/Four_Great_Inventions "Four Great Inventions").[[195]](#cite_note-Yigitcanlar_2016_19-200) The innovations of [Yi Xing](/wiki/Yi_Xing "Yi Xing") (683–727), a polymathic Buddhist monk, mathematician, and mechanical engineer of the Tang dynasty is acknowledged for applying the earliest-known escapement mechanism to a [water-powered celestial globe](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere").[[196]](#cite_note-fry_10-201)[[197]](#cite_note-Needham_volume_3_351-202)[[198]](#cite_note-Needham_volume_4_470-203)[[199]](#cite_note-204) The accomplishments and advancements of the Song dynasty polymath [Su Song](/wiki/Su_Song "Su Song") (1020–1101) is recognized for inventing a [hydro-mechanical](/wiki/Hydraulics "Hydraulics") [astronomical](/wiki/Astronomical_clock "Astronomical clock") [clock tower](/wiki/Clock_tower "Clock tower") in medieval [Kaifeng](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng"), which employed an early [escapement](/wiki/Escapement "Escapement") mechanism.[[200]](#cite_note-Needham_volume_4_445-205)[[201]](#cite_note-Needham_volume_4_448-206)[[202]](#cite_note-bodde_140-207)[[196]](#cite_note-fry_10-201) The work of medieval Chinese polymath [Shen Kuo](/wiki/Shen_Kuo "Shen Kuo") (1031–1095) of the Song dynasty theorized that the sun and moon were spherical and wrote of planetary motions such as retrogradation as well as postulating theories for the processes of geological land formation.[[203]](#cite_note-O'Doherty_57-208) Medieval Han Chinese astronomers were also among the first peoples to record observations of a cosmic [supernova](/wiki/SN_1054 "SN 1054") in 1054 AD, the remnants of which would form the [Crab Nebula](/wiki/Crab_Nebula "Crab Nebula").[[203]](#cite_note-O'Doherty_57-208)

In the contemporary era, Han Chinese have continued to contribute to the development and growth of modern science and technology. Among such prominently illustrious names that have been honored, recognized, remembered, and respected for their historical groundbreaking achievements include [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") laureates [Tu Youyou](/wiki/Tu_Youyou "Tu Youyou"), [Steven Chu](/wiki/Steven_Chu "Steven Chu"), [Samuel C.C. Ting](/wiki/Samuel_C.C._Ting "Samuel C.C. Ting"), [Chen Ning Yang](/wiki/Chen_Ning_Yang "Chen Ning Yang"), [Tsung-Dao Lee](/wiki/Tsung-Dao_Lee "Tsung-Dao Lee"), [Yuan T. Lee](/wiki/Yuan_T._Lee "Yuan T. Lee"), [Daniel C. Tsui](/wiki/Daniel_C._Tsui "Daniel C. Tsui"), [Roger Y. Tsien](/wiki/Roger_Y._Tsien "Roger Y. Tsien") and [Charles K. Kao](/wiki/Charles_K._Kao "Charles K. Kao") (known as the "Godfather of Broadband" and "Father of Fiber Optics");[[204]](#cite_note-209) [Fields Medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medal#List_of_Fields_medalists "Fields Medal") [Terence Tao](/wiki/Terence_Tao "Terence Tao") and [Shing-Tung Yau](/wiki/Shing-Tung_Yau "Shing-Tung Yau") as well as [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") winner [Andrew Yao](/wiki/Andrew_Yao "Andrew Yao"). [Tsien Hsue-shen](/wiki/Tsien_Hsue-shen "Tsien Hsue-shen") was a prominent aerospace engineer who helped to establish [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")'s [Jet Propulsion Laboratory](/wiki/Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory "Jet Propulsion Laboratory").[[205]](#cite_note-210) [Chen Jingrun](/wiki/Chen_Jingrun "Chen Jingrun") was a noted mathematician recognized for his contributions to number theory, where he demonstrated that any sufficiently large even number can be expressed as the sum of two prime numbers or a [prime number](/wiki/Prime_number "Prime number") and a [semiprime](/wiki/Semiprime "Semiprime"), a concept now known as [Chen's theorem](/wiki/Chen%27s_theorem "Chen's theorem").[[206]](#cite_note-211)

The 1978 Wolf Prize in Physics inaugural recipient and physicist [Chien-Shiung Wu](/wiki/Chien-Shiung_Wu "Chien-Shiung Wu"), nicknamed the "First Lady of Physics" contributed to the development of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project") and radically altered modern physical theory and changed the conventionally accepted view of the structure of the universe.[[207]](#cite_note-212) The geometer [Shiing-Shen Chern](/wiki/Shiing-Shen_Chern "Shiing-Shen Chern") has been regarded as the "father of modern differential geometry" and has also been recognized as one of the greatest mathematicians of the 20th century. Chern was awarded the 1984 Wolf Prize in mathematics in recognition for his fundamental contributions to the development and growth of [differential geometry](/wiki/Differential_geometry "Differential geometry") and [topology](/wiki/Topology "Topology").[[208]](#cite_note-213)[[209]](#cite_note-214)[[210]](#cite_note-215)[[211]](#cite_note-216)[[212]](#cite_note-217)[[213]](#cite_note-218)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The botanist [Shang Fa Yang](/wiki/Shang_Fa_Yang "Shang Fa Yang") was well-noted for his research that unlocked the key to prolonging freshness in fruits and flowers and "for his remarkable contributions to the understanding of the mechanism of biosynthesis, mode of action and applications of the plant hormone, [Ethylene](/wiki/Ethylene "Ethylene")."[[214]](#cite_note-219) The agronomist [Yuan Longping](/wiki/Yuan_Longping "Yuan Longping"), regarded as the "Father of Hybrid Rice" was famous for developing the world's first set of [hybrid rice](/wiki/Hybrid_rice "Hybrid rice") varieties in the 1970s, which was then part of the [Green Revolution](/wiki/Green_Revolution "Green Revolution") that marked a major scientific breakthrough within the field of modern agricultural research.[[215]](#cite_note-Economist-obit-220)[[216]](#cite_note-221)[[217]](#cite_note-222)[[218]](#cite_note-223) The physical chemist [Ching W. Tang](/wiki/Ching_W._Tang "Ching W. Tang"), was the inventor of the [organic light-emitting diode](/wiki/Organic_light-emitting_diode "Organic light-emitting diode") (OLED) and hetero-junction [organic photovoltaic cell](/wiki/Organic_photovoltaic_cell "Organic photovoltaic cell") (OPV) and is widely considered the "Father of [Organic Electronics](/wiki/Organic_electronics "Organic electronics")".[[219]](#cite_note-224) Biochemist [Chi-Huey Wong](/wiki/Chi-Huey_Wong "Chi-Huey Wong") is well known for his pioneering research in glycoscience research and developing the first enzymatic method for the large-scale synthesis of oligosaccharides and the first programmable automated synthesis of oligosaccharides. The chemical biologist [Chuan He](/wiki/Chuan_He "Chuan He") is notable for his work in discovering and deciphering reversible [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") methylation in post-transcriptional gene expression regulation.[[220]](#cite_note-Grand_Challenge-225) Chuan is also noteworthy for having invented TAB-seq, a biochemical method that can map 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC) at base-resolution genome-wide, as well as hmC-Seal, a method that covalently labels 5hmC for its detection and profiling.[[221]](#cite_note-Yu_2012-226)[[222]](#cite_note-Song_2011-227)

Other prominent Han Chinese who have made notable contributions the development and growth of modern science and technology include the medical researcher, physician, and virologist [David Ho](/wiki/David_Ho_(scientist) "David Ho (scientist)"), who was one of the first scientists to propose that AIDS was caused by a virus, thus subsequently developing combination antiretroviral therapy to combat it. In recognition of his medical contributions, Ho was named [*Time* magazine Person of the Year](/wiki/Time_Person_of_the_Year "Time Person of the Year") in 1996.[[223]](#cite_note-228) The medical researcher and transplant surgeon [Patrick Soon-Shiong](/wiki/Patrick_Soon-Shiong "Patrick Soon-Shiong") is the inventor of the drug [Abraxane](/wiki/Abraxane "Abraxane"), which became known for its efficacy against lung, breast, and pancreatic cancer.[[224]](#cite_note-Forb_2020/08/27-229) Soon-Shiong is also well known for performing the first whole-pancreas transplant[[225]](#cite_note-UMagUCLA15-230)[[226]](#cite_note-231) and he developed and first performed the experimental [Type 1 diabetes](/wiki/Type_1_diabetes "Type 1 diabetes")-treatment known as encapsulated-human-[islet transplant](/wiki/Islet_transplantation "Islet transplantation"), and the "first pig-to-man islet-cell transplant in diabetic patients."[[225]](#cite_note-UMagUCLA15-230) The physician and physiologist [Thomas Ming Swi Chang](/wiki/Thomas_Ming_Swi_Chang "Thomas Ming Swi Chang") is the inventor of the world's first [artificial cell](/wiki/Artificial_cell "Artificial cell") made from a permeable plastic sack that would effectively carry hemoglobin around the human circulatory system.[[227]](#cite_note-pioneer-232) Chang is also noteworthy for his development of charcoal-filled cells to treat drug poisoning in addition to the discovery of enzymes carried by artificial cells as a medical tool to correct the faults within some metabolic disorders.[[228]](#cite_note-233) [Min Chueh Chang](/wiki/Min_Chueh_Chang "Min Chueh Chang") was the co-inventor of the [combined oral contraceptive pill](/wiki/Combined_oral_contraceptive_pill "Combined oral contraceptive pill") and is known for his pioneering work and significant contributions to the development of [in vitro fertilization](/wiki/In_vitro_fertilization "In vitro fertilization") at the [Worcester Foundation for Experimental Biology](/wiki/Worcester_Foundation_for_Experimental_Biology "Worcester Foundation for Experimental Biology").[[229]](#cite_note-234)[[230]](#cite_note-235) Biochemist [Choh Hao Li](/wiki/Choh_Hao_Li "Choh Hao Li") discovered [human growth hormone](/wiki/Growth_hormone "Growth hormone") (and subsequently used it to treat a form of [dwarfism](/wiki/Dwarfism "Dwarfism") caused by [growth hormone deficiency](/wiki/Growth_hormone_deficiency "Growth hormone deficiency")), [beta-endorphin](/wiki/Beta-endorphin "Beta-endorphin") (the most powerful of the body's natural painkillers), [follicle-stimulating hormone](/wiki/Follicle-stimulating_hormone "Follicle-stimulating hormone") and [luteinizing hormone](/wiki/Luteinizing_hormone "Luteinizing hormone") (the key hormone used in [fertility testing](/wiki/Fertility_testing "Fertility testing"), an example is the [ovulation](/wiki/Ovulation "Ovulation") home test).[[231]](#cite_note-236)[[232]](#cite_note-CHL-237) [Joe Hin Tjio](/wiki/Joe_Hin_Tjio "Joe Hin Tjio") was a cytogeneticist renowned as the first person to recognize the normal number of human chromosomes, a breakthrough in [karyotype](/wiki/Karyotype "Karyotype") [genetics](/wiki/Cytogenetics "Cytogenetics").[[233]](#cite_note-238)[[234]](#cite_note-239) The bio-engineer [Yuan-Cheng Fung](/wiki/Yuan-Cheng_Fung "Yuan-Cheng Fung"), was regarded as the "Father of modern [biomechanics](/wiki/Biomechanics "Biomechanics")" for pioneering the application of quantitative and analytical engineering principles to the study of the human body and disease.[[235]](#cite_note-240)[[236]](#cite_note-241) China's system of "[barefoot doctors](/wiki/Barefoot_doctors "Barefoot doctors")" was among the most important inspirations for the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") conference in Alma Ata, Kazakhstan in 1978, and was hailed as a revolutionary breakthrough in international health ideology emphasizing [primary health care](/wiki/Primary_health_care "Primary health care") and [preventive medicine](/wiki/Preventive_medicine "Preventive medicine").[[237]](#cite_note-242)[[238]](#cite_note-243)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main articles: [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"), [Three teachings](/wiki/Three_teachings "Three teachings"), [Chinese folk religion in Southeast Asia](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion_in_Southeast_Asia "Chinese folk religion in Southeast Asia"), and [Religion in China](/wiki/Religion_in_China "Religion in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Wudang_Mountain_%2854131067531%29.jpg/250px-Wudang_Mountain_%2854131067531%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wudang_Mountain_(54131067531).jpg)

[Taoist](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") temple on top of [Wudang Mountains](/wiki/Wudang_Mountains "Wudang Mountains") in [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei"), [China](/wiki/China "China").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Confucius_Laozi_Buddha.jpg/250px-Confucius_Laozi_Buddha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Confucius_Laozi_Buddha.jpg)

[Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius") handing over an infant [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha") to an elderly [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi").

[Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), and [Chinese Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"), as well as other various traditional homegrown Chinese philosophies, have influenced not only Han Chinese culture, but also the [neighboring cultures in East Asia](/wiki/East_Asian_religions "East Asian religions"). Chinese spiritual culture has been long characterized by [religious pluralism](/wiki/Religious_pluralism "Religious pluralism") and [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") has always maintained a profound influence within the confines of Chinese civilization both historically and in the modern era. Indigenous Confucianism and Taoism share aspects of being a philosophy or a religion and neither demand exclusive adherence, resulting in a culture of tolerance and [syncretism](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism"), where multiple religions or belief systems are often practiced in conjunction with local customs and traditions. Han culture has for long been influenced by [Mahayana Buddhism](/wiki/Mahayana_Buddhism "Mahayana Buddhism"), while in recent centuries [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_China "Christianity in China") has also gained a foothold among the population.[[239]](#cite_note-244)

Chinese folk religion is a set of worship traditions of the [ethnic deities](/wiki/Shen_(Chinese_religion) "Shen (Chinese religion)") of the Han people. It involves the worship of various extraordinary figures in [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology "Chinese mythology") and [history](/wiki/History_of_China "History of China"), heroic personnel such as [Guan Yu](/wiki/Guan_Yu "Guan Yu") and [Qu Yuan](/wiki/Qu_Yuan "Qu Yuan"), mythological creatures such as the [Chinese dragon](/wiki/Chinese_dragon "Chinese dragon") or family, clan and national ancestors. These practices vary from region to region and do not characterize an organized religion, though many [traditional Chinese holidays](/wiki/List_of_observances_set_by_the_Chinese_calendar "List of observances set by the Chinese calendar") such as the [Duanwu (or Dragon Boat) Festival](/wiki/Dragon_Boat_Festival "Dragon Boat Festival"), [Qingming Festival](/wiki/Qingming_Festival "Qingming Festival"), [Zhongyuan Festival](/wiki/Ghost_Festival "Ghost Festival") and the [Mid-Autumn Festival](/wiki/Mid-Autumn_Festival "Mid-Autumn Festival") come from the most popular of these traditions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), another [indigenous Han philosophy and religion](/wiki/Indigenous_religion "Indigenous religion"), is also widely practiced by the Han in both its folk forms and as an organized religion with its traditions having been a source of vestigial perennial influence on Chinese art, poetry, philosophy, [music](/wiki/Taoist_music "Taoist music"), [medicine](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_medicine "Traditional Chinese medicine"), [astronomy](/wiki/Chinese_astronomy "Chinese astronomy"), [Neidan](/wiki/Neidan "Neidan") and [alchemy](/wiki/Chinese_alchemy "Chinese alchemy"), [dietary habits](/wiki/Taoist_diet "Taoist diet"), [Neijia](/wiki/Neijia "Neijia") and other [martial arts](/wiki/Chinese_martial_arts "Chinese martial arts") and [architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture "Chinese architecture"). Taoism was the state religion during the Han and Tang eras where it also often enjoyed state patronage under subsequent emperors and successive ruling dynasties.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Confucianism, although sometimes described as a religion, is another indigenous governing philosophy and moral code with some religious elements like ancestor worship. It continues to be deeply ingrained in modern Chinese culture and was the official state philosophy in ancient China during the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") and until the [fall of imperial China in the 20th century](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Qing_dynasty "Fall of the Qing dynasty") (though it is worth noting that there is a [movement in China today advocating that the culture be "re-Confucianized")](/wiki/New_Confucianism "New Confucianism").[[240]](#cite_note-245)

During the Han dynasty, [Confucian](/wiki/Confucian "Confucian") ideals were the dominant ideology. Near the end of the dynasty, Buddhism entered China, later gaining popularity. Historically, Buddhism alternated between periods of state tolerance (and even patronage) and [persecution](/wiki/Four_Buddhist_Persecutions_in_China "Four Buddhist Persecutions in China"). In its original form, certain ideas in [Buddhism](/wiki/Pre-sectarian_Buddhism "Pre-sectarian Buddhism") was not quite compatible with traditional Chinese cultural values, especially with the Confucian sociopolitical elite, as certain Buddhist values conflicted with Chinese sensibilities. However, through centuries of mutual tolerance, assimilation, adaptation, and syncretism, [Chinese Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism") gained a respectable place in the culture. Chinese Buddhism was also influenced by Confucianism and Taoism and exerted influence in turn – such as in the form of [Neo-Confucianism](/wiki/Neo-Confucianism "Neo-Confucianism") and Buddhist influences in Chinese folk religion, such as the cult of Guanyin, who is treated as a Bodhisattva, immortal, goddess or exemplar of Confucian virtue, depending on the tradition. The four largest schools of Han Buddhism (Chan, Jingtu, Tiantai and Huayan) were all developed in China and later spread throughout the Chinese sphere of influence.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Though [Christian influence in China](/wiki/Christianity_in_China "Christianity in China") existed as early as the 7th century, Christianity did not gain a significant foothold in China until the [establishment of contact with Europeans](/wiki/Europeans_in_Medieval_China "Europeans in Medieval China") during the [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") and [Qing dynasties](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"). Christian beliefs often had conflicts with traditional Chinese values and customs which eventually resulted in the [Chinese Rites controversy](/wiki/Chinese_Rites_controversy "Chinese Rites controversy") and a subsequent reduction in Christian influence in the country. Christianity grew considerably following the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), after which foreign missionaries in China enjoyed the protection of the Western powers and engaged in widespread proselytizing.[[241]](#cite_note-246)

The People's Republic of China government defined Chinese-speaking Muslims as a separate ethnic group, the "[Hui People](/wiki/Hui_people "Hui people")". This was opposed by the Republic of China government and Muslim celebrities such as [Bai Chongxi](/wiki/Bai_Chongxi "Bai Chongxi"), the founder of the [Chinese Muslim Association](/wiki/Chinese_Muslim_Association "Chinese Muslim Association"). Han Chinese Muslims were categorised as "[inland nationals with special living customs](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%AC%AC%E4%B8%80%E5%B1%86%E4%B8%AD%E8%8F%AF%E6%B0%91%E5%9C%8B%E5%9C%8B%E6%B0%91%E5%A4%A7%E6%9C%83%E4%BB%A3%E8%A1%A8#內地生活習慣特殊國民代表 "zh:第一屆中華民國國民大會代表")" under the Republic of China government.[[242]](#cite_note-247) Bai Chongxi believed that "Hui" is an alternative name for [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_China "Islam in China") as a religion in the Chinese language instead of the name for any ethnic group, and that Chinese-speaking Muslims should not be considered as a separate ethnic group apart from other Han Chinese.[[243]](#cite_note-248)

## Genetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Genetics")]

Main articles: [Genetic history of East Asians § Han Chinese](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_East_Asians#Han_Chinese "Genetic history of East Asians"), and [Peopling of China](/wiki/Peopling_of_China "Peopling of China")

### Internal genetic structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Internal genetic structure")]

The internal genetic structure of the Han Chinese is consistent with the vast geographical expanse of China. The reference population for the Han Chinese used in [Geno 2.0 Next Generation](/wiki/Genographic_Project#Geno_2.0_Next_Generation "Genographic Project") is 81% Eastern Asia, 2% Finland and Northern Siberia, 8% Central Asia, and 7% Southeast Asia & Oceania.[[244]](#cite_note-249) The recorded history of large [migratory waves](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_dynasties#Demographics "Northern and Southern dynasties") over the past several millennia have also engendered the emergence of diverse [Han subgroups](/wiki/Han_Chinese_subgroups "Han Chinese subgroups"), who display slight but discernible physical and physiological differences. Although genetically similar, Han Chinese subgroups exhibit a north–south stratification in their genetics,[[245]](#cite_note-GS-250)[[246]](#cite_note-HC-251)[[247]](#cite_note-Genetic_evidence_supports_demic_dif-252)[[248]](#cite_note-PP-253) with centrally placed populations acting as conduits for outlying ones.[[245]](#cite_note-GS-250) Despite no clear genetic divide between the north and south due to the Han Chinese being a clinal population, many studies simply categorize the Han Chinese into two subgroups out of convenience: Northern and Southern Han Chinese.[[249]](#cite_note-254)

Several genetic studies show that both Northern and Southern Han Chinese share ancestry with [Neolithic Chinese populations from the Central Plains](/wiki/Peopling_of_China#Archaeogenetic_studies_in_the_Central_Plains "Peopling of China").[[250]](#cite_note-255)[[251]](#cite_note-256)[[252]](#cite_note-257)[[253]](#cite_note-258) Northern Han Chinese and Southern Han Chinese can be modeled as having Neolithic Yellow River ([Sino-Tibetan](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_peoples "Sino-Tibetan peoples")) and [Kra-Dai](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai-speaking_peoples "Kra–Dai-speaking peoples") ancestries, although Kra-Dai ancestry is more common in Southern Han.[[254]](#cite_note-:0-259)[[255]](#cite_note-:422-260) Despite shared Neolithic Yellow River ancestry, Han sub-groups slightly differ in their ancestral components, reflective of their vast demographic history. Specifically, they tend to share some maternal ancestry with geographically close minority groups. For example, Southern Han show evidence of being admixed with populations of Tai-Kadai and Austronesian ancestry. Southwestern Han show admixture with [Hmong-Mien](/wiki/Hmong_people "Hmong people") speakers, whilst Northwestern Han have very minor West Eurasian ancestral components, dating 4,500–1,200/1,300 years ago. Northeastern Han have more Yellow River Basin and Ancient Northeast Asian ancestry than Southern Han Chinese.[[256]](#cite_note-261) According to a 2025 study, modern Han Chinese are the most related to Yellow River populations from the Middle Neolithic period.[[257]](#cite_note-:9-262)

Variation notwithstanding, Han Chinese subgroups are genetically closer to each other than they each are to their Korean and Yamato neighbors,[[258]](#cite_note-pmid_29636655-263) to whom they are also genetically close in general. The close genetic relationship between the Han across the entirety of China has led to their characterization as having a "coherent genetic structure".[[246]](#cite_note-HC-251)[[248]](#cite_note-PP-253)

The two notable exceptions to this structure are [Pinghua](/wiki/Pinghua "Pinghua") and [Tanka people](/wiki/Tanka_people "Tanka people"),[[259]](#cite_note-264) who on their patrilines, bear a closer genetic resemblance to aboriginal peoples, but have Han matrilines. The Tanka are a group of boat-dwellers who speak a Sinitic language and who claim Han ancestry, but who have traditionally faced severe discrimination from the other Southern Han subgroups. Unlike the Guangdong, Fujian and Hainan Han (whose dominant Y-chromosome haplotype is the Han patriline O2-M122), the Tanka have been shown instead to have a predominantly non-Han patriline similar to Daic peoples from Guizhou.[[260]](#cite_note-UG-265)[[261]](#cite_note-GF-266) However, matrilineally, the Tanka are closely clustered with the Hakka Han and Teochew Han, rather than with Austronesian or Austroasiatic populations, thus supporting an admixture hypothesis and validating, even if only partially, their own claims to Han ancestry.[[260]](#cite_note-UG-265)[[261]](#cite_note-GF-266)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Han_chinese_genetic_0.png/500px-Han_chinese_genetic_0.png)](/wiki/File:Han_chinese_genetic_0.png)

A PCA graph illustrates the genetic differences among Han Chinese subgroups.[[262]](#cite_note-267)

### Demic diffusion and north–south differences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Demic diffusion and north–south differences")]

The estimated contribution of Northern Han to Southern Han is substantial in both paternal and maternal lineages and a geographic [cline](/wiki/Cline_(biology) "Cline (biology)") exists for mtDNA. As a result, the Northern Han are one of the primary contributors to the gene pool of the Southern Han. However, it is noteworthy that the expansion process was not only dominated by males, as is shown by both contribution of the Y-chromosome and the mtDNA from Northern Han to Southern Han. Northern Han Chinese and Southern Han Chinese exhibit both Ancient Northern East Asian and Ancient Southern East Asian ancestries.[[263]](#cite_note-268)
[[264]](#cite_note-Ancient_Jomon_genome_sequence_analy-269) The subsequent intermarriages between Northern Han migrants and southern aborigines over the past few thousand years gave rise to modern Chinese demographics—a Han Chinese super-majority and minority non-Han Chinese indigenous peoples.[[247]](#cite_note-Genetic_evidence_supports_demic_dif-252)

Han Chinese from Fujian and Guangdong show excessive ancestries from [Late Neolithic Fujianese](/wiki/Peopling_of_China#Archaeogenetic_studies_in_southern_China "Peopling of China")-related sources (35.0–40.3%), which are more significant in modern [Ami](/wiki/Amis_people "Amis people"), [Atayal](/wiki/Atayal_people "Atayal people") and [Kankanaey](/wiki/Kankanaey_people "Kankanaey people") (66.9–74.3%), and less significant in Han Chinese from Zhejiang (22%), Jiangsu (17%) and Shandong (8%). This suggests significant genetic contribution from Kra-Dai-related peoples. They also have ancestry from [Neolithic Mekong](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages#Austroasiatic_migrations_and_archaeogenetics "Austroasiatic languages")-related sources but this is less significant (21.8–23.6%). Among the Han subgroups, Han Chinese from Guangxi exhibit the lowest northern East Asian ancestry (33.8 ± 4.8%)[[265]](#cite_note-:4-270) although other studies suggest Cantonese, Fujianese and Taiwanese Han.[[266]](#cite_note-271)[[267]](#cite_note-272)[[268]](#cite_note-273)[[269]](#cite_note-Detecting_Genetic_Ancestry_and_Adap-274) Conversely, Han Chinese from [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi") and [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia") have exceptionally high northern East Asian ancestry although much less than neighboring northern Chinese minorities.[[270]](#cite_note-275)[[271]](#cite_note-276) Han Chinese from Guangxi and Hainan cluster with Han Chinese from Guangdong but exhibit admixture with minority groups from their respective provinces.[[272]](#cite_note-:5-277)[[273]](#cite_note-278)[[274]](#cite_note-:1-279) One study shows higher affinities between Han Chinese from Northern Guangxi and local Austronesian, Kra-Dai and Austroasiatic groups compared to Han Chinese from Southern Guangxi.[[274]](#cite_note-:1-279) Another study shows stronger affinities between Han Chinese from Hainan and [Tujia](/wiki/Tujia_people "Tujia people"), [Bai](/wiki/Bai_people "Bai people"), [She](/wiki/She_people "She people"), [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") [Yi](/wiki/Yi_people "Yi people") and Sinitic-speaking populations compared to indigenous Hlai peoples, who show more Kra-Dai affinities.[[275]](#cite_note-280) [Lingnan](/wiki/Lingnan "Lingnan") Han Chinese also share affinities with the Kinh Vietnamese,[[272]](#cite_note-:5-277) although other studies show stronger affinities between [Dai people](/wiki/Dai_people "Dai people") and Kinh Vietnamese.[[276]](#cite_note-281)[[277]](#cite_note-:7-282)[[278]](#cite_note-:8-283) Ancient population admixture with Ami and Atayal exists for Han Chinese from Guangdong and [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan")[[279]](#cite_note-284)[[269]](#cite_note-Detecting_Genetic_Ancestry_and_Adap-274) and the ancestors of [Taiwanese Han](/wiki/Taiwanese_Han "Taiwanese Han").[[269]](#cite_note-Detecting_Genetic_Ancestry_and_Adap-274)

In contrast, Southwestern Han Chinese exhibit admixture with neighboring Hmong-Mien-speaking and lowland [Tibeto-Burman](/wiki/Tibeto-Burman_languages "Tibeto-Burman languages")-speaking populations[[280]](#cite_note-285) and have higher northern East Asian affinities.[[281]](#cite_note-286) Highland Tibetan-related ancestry is also detected in some Northern and Central Han Chinese.[[282]](#cite_note-287)[[283]](#cite_note-288) Eastern Han Chinese from provinces like Jiangsu, Shanghai, Zhejiang and Shandong have Yellow River ancestry and to a lesser extent, southern East Asian-related ancestry. However, Han Chinese from Shandong derive their ancestries from populations with higher northern East Asian affinities.[[284]](#cite_note-289)

#### Patrilineal DNA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Patrilineal DNA")]

Typical Y-DNA haplogroups of present-day Han Chinese include Haplogroup O-M95, [Haplogroup O-M122](/wiki/Haplogroup_O-M122 "Haplogroup O-M122"), [Haplogroup O-M175](/wiki/Haplogroup_O-M175 "Haplogroup O-M175"), [Haplogroup C-M130](/wiki/Haplogroup_C-M130 "Haplogroup C-M130"), [Haplogroup N-M231](/wiki/Haplogroup_N-M231 "Haplogroup N-M231") and [Haplogroup Q-M120](/wiki/Haplogroup_Q-M120 "Haplogroup Q-M120").[[285]](#cite_note-AD-290)

The Y-chromosome haplogroup distribution between Southern Han Chinese and Northern Han Chinese populations and principal core component analysis indicates that almost all modern Han Chinese populations form a tight cluster in their Y chromosome:

* Haplogroups prevalent in non-Han southern natives such as O1b-M110, O2a1-M88 and O3d-M7, which are prevalent in non-Han southern natives, were observed in 4% of Southern Han Chinese and not at all in the Northern Han.[[286]](#cite_note-table-291)[[287]](#cite_note-EJH-292)
* Biological research findings have also demonstrated that the paternal lineages Y-DNA O-M119,[[288]](#cite_note-Li_et_al_(2008)-293) O-P201,[[289]](#cite_note-Karafet_et_al_(2010)-294) O-P203[[289]](#cite_note-Karafet_et_al_(2010)-294) and O-M95[[290]](#cite_note-Karafet_et_al_2005-295) are found in commonly Southern non-Han minorities, less commonly in Southern Han, and even less frequently in Northern Han Chinese.[[291]](#cite_note-Yan_et_al_(2011)-296)
* Haplogroups O1 and O2 significantly peak in the southeastern coastlines and eastern regions of China respectively, according to one study.[[292]](#cite_note-297)

Patrilineal DNA indicates the Northern Han Chinese were the primary contributors to the paternal gene pool of modern southern Han Chinese.[[285]](#cite_note-AD-290)[[286]](#cite_note-table-291)[[287]](#cite_note-EJH-292)[[291]](#cite_note-Yan_et_al_(2011)-296) The data also indicates that the contribution of southern non-Han aboriginals to the southern Han Chinese genetics is limited. In short, male Han Chinese were the primary drivers of Han Chinese expansion in successive migratory waves from the north into what is now modern Southern China as is shown by a greater contribution to the Y-chromosome than the mtDNA from northern to Southern Han.[[247]](#cite_note-Genetic_evidence_supports_demic_dif-252)

During the Zhou dynasty, or earlier, peoples with haplogroup Q-M120 also contributed to the ethnogenesis of Han Chinese people. This haplogroup is implied to be spread across in the Eurasian steppe and north Asia since it is found among [Cimmerians](/wiki/Cimmerians "Cimmerians") in [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova") and Bronze Age natives of [Khövsgöl](/wiki/Kh%C3%B6vsg%C3%B6l_Province "Khövsgöl Province"). But it is currently near-absent in these regions except for East Asia. In modern China, haplogroup Q-M120 can be found in the northern and eastern regions.[[293]](#cite_note-298)

Sub-lineages of haplogroups C2b, O2a2a and O1b-M268 are common for populations from Eastern China. In particular, many individuals from Eastern China have haplogroups related to O1b1a2. This haplogroup is quite rare in East Asia but is mostly found in the southeastern part of Northeastern China and Vietnam, especially among [Han Chinese individuals](/wiki/Hoa_people "Hoa people").[[294]](#cite_note-299)

**Matrilineal DNA**

MtDNA of Han Chinese increases in diversity as one looks from northern to Southern China, which suggests that the influx of male Han Chinese migrants intermarried with the local female non-Han aborigines after arriving in what is now modern-day Fujian, Guangdong, Guangxi, Hainan and other regions of Southern China.[[286]](#cite_note-table-291)[[287]](#cite_note-EJH-292) In these populations, the contribution to mtDNA from Han Chinese and indigenous tribes is evenly matched, representing a substantial mtDNA contribution from non-Han groups, collectively known as the Bai Yue or Hundred Yue.[[286]](#cite_note-table-291)[[287]](#cite_note-EJH-292)

A study by the Chinese Academy of Sciences into the gene frequency data of Han sub-populations and ethnic minorities in China, showed that Han sub-populations in different regions are also genetically quite close to the local ethnic non-Han minorities, meaning that in many cases, the blood of ethnic minorities had mixed into Han genetic substrate through varying degrees of intermarriage, while at the same time, the blood of the Han had also mixed into the genetic substrates of the local ethnic non-Han minorities.[[295]](#cite_note-300)

### Genetic continuity between ancient and modern Han Chinese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Genetic continuity between ancient and modern Han Chinese")]

The Hengbei archaeological site in Jiang County, southern Shanxi was part of the suburbs of the capital during the Zhou dynasty. Genetic material from human remains in Hengbei have been used to examine the genetic continuity between ancient and modern Han Chinese.[[246]](#cite_note-HC-251)

Comparisons of Y chromosome [single-nucleotide polymorphisms](/wiki/Single-nucleotide_polymorphism "Single-nucleotide polymorphism") (SNPs) and [mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") (mtDNA) between modern Northern Han Chinese and 3,000-year-old Hengbei samples reveal extreme similarity, confirming genetic continuity between ancient Hengbei inhabitants to present-day Northern Han Chinese. This shows that the core genetic structure of Northern Han Chinese was established more than three thousand years ago in the Central Plains Area.[[246]](#cite_note-HC-251)[[296]](#cite_note-301) Additionally, these studies indicate that contemporary northern and southern Han Chinese populations exhibit an almost identical Y-DNA genetic structure, indicating a common paternal descent, corroborating the historical record of Han Chinese migration to the south.[[246]](#cite_note-HC-251) However, a study of [mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") from Yinxu commoner graves in the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") showed similarity with modern Northern Han Chinese, but significant differences from southern Han Chinese, indicating admixture on the matriline.[[297]](#cite_note-302)

About 2,000 years ago, between the Warring States period and Eastern Han dynasty, the northeast coastlines of China faced an eastward migration from the Central Plains, shaping the genetic structure of local populations to the present. These populations also have more southern East Asian ancestry compared to their predecessors.[[298]](#cite_note-303)

### Closely related East Asian groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Closely related East Asian groups")]

The Han Chinese show a close yet distinguishable genetic relationship with other East Asian populations such as the [Koreans](/wiki/Koreans "Koreans") and [Yamato](/wiki/Yamato_people "Yamato people").[[299]](#cite_note-304)[[258]](#cite_note-pmid_29636655-263) Although the genetic relationship is close, the various Han Chinese subgroups are genetically closer to each other than to their Korean and Japanese counterparts.[[258]](#cite_note-pmid_29636655-263)

Other research suggests a significant overlap between Yamato Japanese and the Northern Han Chinese in particular.[[300]](#cite_note-305)

## Criticisms of the term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Criticisms of the term")]

### Taxonomic qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Taxonomic qualification")]

The classification of Han Chinese has gone through numerous historical permutations. This, coupled with the loose terms of Han qualification, has led some modern academics to question its use both historically and modernly. It has been critiqued by some as being used similarly to the concept of "whiteness" in the Western tradition,[[301]](#cite_note-306) expanding and contrasting to include whoever is politically useful.[[302]](#cite_note-307) This has led to a relatively recent resurgence in viewing the concept of Han Chinese through a critical lens.[[303]](#cite_note-308)[[304]](#cite_note-:3-309)

> "Han Chinese, as a category, holds considerable commonality with the category of whiteness. Both are comparatively recent social constructions that fused together large subgroups of people, who themselves possessed their own distinct cultures and languages. Although Han Chinese as a defined ethno-racial category did not exist prior to the 20th century, 'the modern ethnonym builds on a much older historical formulation, one that informs the "cultural stuff" which defines the boundaries, symbols and sentiment of Han today'." - *The operations of contemporary Han Chinese privilege*, Reza Hasmath[[305]](#cite_note-310)

### Nationalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Han_Chinese&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Nationalism")]

Some critics argue that term has been particularly used as a tool of [Chinese populist nationalism](/wiki/Chinese_nationalism#Populist_nationalism "Chinese nationalism") at the end of the Qing dynasty.[[304]](#cite_note-:3-309) This phenomenon is called [Han Nationalism](/wiki/Han_nationalism "Han nationalism"). The [Xinhai revolution](/wiki/Xinhai_revolution "Xinhai revolution") of 1911 saw the rise of Chinese nationalism as a primary competing ideology of the 20th century after its use in establishing the Republic as a populist, [anti-Qing](/wiki/Anti-Qing_sentiment "Anti-Qing sentiment")[[f]](#cite_note-311) rallying cry. Though after the Republic was established there were policies to progressively ease racial tensions, such as the doctrine of [Five Races Under One Union](/wiki/Five_Races_Under_One_Union "Five Races Under One Union") (五族共和; wǔ zú gònghé; literally "five-race republic"), Han Nationalism as a movement has remained to the modern day.[[304]](#cite_note-:3-309)

5. **[^](#cite_ref-Xia_147-0)** There has been a lot of modern discourse relating to the Xia dynasty, and its possible role in the greater movements of Chinese history. It is generally accepted to have been at least largely legendary by modern scholarship. The modern consensus, formed primarily from a lack of archaeological evidence and any lack of mention during the Shang dynasty, would likely indicate it was a construction during the Zhou dynasty after the Zhou conquest of the Shang dynasty. This was most likely in order to establish a precedent that would retroactively justify Zhou rule. Any historical form that the possible historical Xia peoples would have existed would have little resemblance to a unified civilization and more of that of a loose band of tribes around the Yellow River valley. The legendary status of the Xia would then give similar justification when the Zhou dynastic rule is ended to pave way for the Han, and then by course all other major dynasties until 1911. The legend of the Xia is largely necessary to traditional narratives of the Mandate of Heaven, and without it the narrative would become unstable due to a regress.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-311)** By virtue of being an ethnic supremacist ideology, it naturally positions itself against the Manchu's, the ethnic rulers of the Qing dynasty.

226. **[^](#cite_ref-231)** Whole [pancreas transplantation](/wiki/Pancreas_transplantation "Pancreas transplantation") began as a part of multi-organ transplants, in the mid-to-late 1960s, at the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota"). See Squifflet, J.P.; Gruessner, R.W.; Sutherland, D.E. (2008). "The History of Pancreas Transplantation: Past, Present and Future". *Acta Chir. Belg*. **108** (3, May–June): 367–378. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1080/00015458.2008.11680243](https://doi.org/10.1080%2F00015458.2008.11680243). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [18710120](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18710120). "The first attempt to cure type 1 diabetes by pancreas transplantation was done at the University of Minnesota, in Minneapolis, on December 17, 1966... [This] opened the door to a period, between the mid-[1970s] to mid-[1980s] where only segmental pancreatic grafts were used... In the late [1970s] – early [1980s], three major events... boosted the development of pancreas transplantation... [At] the Spitzingsee meetings, participants had the idea to renew the urinary drainage technique of the exocrine secretion of the pancreatic graft with segmental graft and eventually with whole pancreaticoduodenal transplant. That was clinically achieved during the mid-[1980s] and remained the mainstay technique during the next decade. In parallel, the Swedish group developed the whole pancreas transplantation technique with enteric diversion. It was the onset of the whole pancreas reign. The enthusiasm for the technique was rather moderated in its early phase due to the rapid development of liver transplantation and the need for sharing vascular structures between both organs, liver and pancreas. During the modern era of immunosuppression, the whole pancreas transplantation technique with enteric diversion became the gold standard... [for SPK, PAK, PTA]."
